schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Agriculture and chemicalization: Perspectives in a rapidly changing field
schedule_event.description:
Global agriculture has been transformed in recent decades through processes of agricultural intensification, corporate restructuring and consolidation, and deepening chemical dependence. Within this transformation, pesticides have ascended from mere farm inputs to occupy a central role in modern agri-food systems. They now function not only as technical inputs but also as key commodities for capital accumulation, state power, and integration into volatile global markets (Berndt et al., 2025; Mansfield et al., 2023). Existing research has highlighted how these processes are unevenly embedded across regions and scales, reshaping agrarian livelihoods, ecological relations, and agrarian politics, while often reproducing or intensifying social, economic, and environmental inequalities (Werner, Berndt and Mansfield, 2022). Global agrochemical value chains have been reconfigured, shifting corporate concentration and reinforcing asymmetric power relations between farmers, states, and markets. The ongoing chemicalization of agriculture, combined with these power dynamics, renders the so-called 'global pesticide complex' an ever-evolving and rapidly transforming field (Mansfield et al., 2023; Werner, 2025). The session will also explore alternative agricultures, food sovereignty, and agroecological proposals that have been contesting the imperative of corporate profit.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Map Room Conversations (3): Re-Thinking Indian Map Traditions
schedule_event.description: Maps establish conversations between spaces, bringing into dialogue locations, symbols, meaning, and people. The relation between mapping as practice and maps as objects has long been the subject of theoretical and methodological debate within and beyond the discipline of geography. In the sessions below we use the RGS-IBG map collection to continue this debate about maps and mapping through two types of conversation (which build on the first sessions of this type at the 2024 conference, see https://rgs-ibg.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/toc/10.1111/(ISSN)1475-4762.map-room-conv). The first is between two researchers who use maps from the collection to explore a shared interest from different perspectives. The second conversation will be between the researchers, who will present some of the maps they work with, and the participants in these interactive sessions. We invite you to take this opportunity for close encounters and conversations with the Society’s map collection.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Emerging Methods and Approaches for Researching Internet Infrastructures
schedule_event.description: In recent years, various academic disciplines have developed a renewed interest in the geographies of Internet infrastructures. This has drawn attention to the often overlooked infrastructural conditions of data flows and their political embeddedness. For example, studies on the geographies of digital network interconnection discuss the importance of urban (colocation) data centres for international data traffic (Monstadt & Saltzman, 2025; Rosa, 2021), work on the geographies of submarine cables, landing stations and Internet shutdowns describe postcolonial path dependencies in transcontinental data flows (Dambrosio et al., 2025; Grover, 2023; Mwema & Birhane, 2024;), studies on the geographies of network topologies reveal state measures of national data isolation or control (Douzet et al., 2022; Limonier et al., 2021; Salamatian et al., 2021), and work on the geographies of digital platforms discuss the close future of a spatially fragmented and simultaneously centralised (post-)Internet (Huston, 2023; Stocker et al., 2021). At the same time, one of the greatest challenges for critical social science research continues to be the challenging empirical and methodological approaches to these often „invisible“ geographies (Furlong, 2021). In this session, we therefore would like to bring together specific methodological and methodological-conceptual approaches for further research on Internet infrastructures. We are interested in both more ‘quantitative’ methods, such as those related to computer sciences and Internet measurements, and more ‘qualitative’ and ethnographic approaches, as well as methods for mapping physical Internet infrastructures. In addition, we welcome ideas for contributions that address broader methodological reflections or establish disciplinary historical references to research methods on Internet infrastructures.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Designing Pluriversality in Times of Crisis: Envisioning Spatial Justice Through Alternative Cartographies (2)
schedule_event.description:
What happens when crises become mappable?

Contemporary societies are increasingly shaped by crisis as an everyday condition rather than an exceptional event. Environmental contamination, climate risks, infrastructural failures, humanitarian displacements - to mention a few - unfold through a mix of visible emergencies and less visible, slow, and unevenly distributed forms of harm. These crises are not only experienced on the ground; they are continuously identified, stabilised, and rendered governable through visual systems.

Maps, dashboards, and visual models have become central to how crises are recognised, communicated, and acted upon. From pollution alerts and flood forecasts to humanitarian risk maps and urban performance indicators, crisis cartographies translate complex, uncertain, and often contested conditions into spatial forms that can circulate across institutions, policy arenas, and publics. In doing so, they do more than depict crises. They shape how crisis is defined, where it is located, whose experiences are counted, and which responses appear legitimate or necessary.

These mapping practices are deeply implicated in the production and governance of inequality. Crisis cartographies rely on classificatory schemes, thresholds, and visual conventions that privilege certain forms of knowledge and render others marginal or invisible. While often mobilised in the name of evidence-based decision-making, dominant visual approaches can flatten lived experiences, obscure historical responsibility, and reproduce epistemic and spatial injustices.

We approach mapping as a political, ethical, and relational practice rather than a neutral technique. The session highlights how cartographies operate not only as representations but also as methods, infrastructures, and interventions that influence how crises and inequalities are understood and addressed. It asks how geographers might rethink mapping practices to support more just and plural spatial futures, within and beyond institutional frameworks.

We welcome empirical, conceptual, and methodological papers engaging with themes including:

• Crisis cartographies as visual objects, examining how maps, dashboards, and visual models shape what is seen, valued, and governed

• Crisis cartographies as methods, focusing on mapping as a process of sensing, translating, and maintaining crisis data and experiences, including relational or low tech approaches

• Crisis cartographies as interventions, exploring how mapping participates in political struggles, institutional decision making, or collective action

• Crisis cartographies as infrastructures, attending to the socio technical systems, platforms, policies, and labour that sustain mapping practices over time

• How mapping practices shape the recognition and governance of inequality, and whose experiences are made visible or left out

• Alternative cartographic practices, such as participatory, feminist, decolonial, artistic, or speculative mapping, that rework dominant visual orders and open possibilities for spatial justice.

paper.title: Alter-Mapping Crisis and Latency. Territorial delay, infrastructural hierarchies and socio-ecological futures

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Bridging Urban (and) Food Studies through Infrastructure and Governance Research
schedule_event.description:
Urban food has become a key area of inquiry and a strategic lever in academic and practitioner communities seeking to transform food systems. Recent compilations in the field identify three major advancements: more systemic engagement with the food system, greater attention to scalar complexity, and a stronger focus on relational governance dynamics; while also pointing out the need to further strengthen conceptual clarity around the urban, governance and policy, and issues of power and inequality (Clark et al., 2021; Hayden & Martín, 2025; Moragues-Faus et al., 2024; Moragues-Faus & Battersby, 2021).

As urban food scholarship continues expanding, there has been increasing interest in the interactions between food systems and other socio-ecological systems shaping food security and sustainability outcomes as well as urban life (Battersby et al., 2024). Addressing these dynamics requires greater dialogue between urban and food studies, building on their transdisciplinary foundations. One emerging contribution to this effort is urban food infrastructures research - inspired by the infrastructural turn in urban studies (see Amin, 2014; Campbell et al., 2022; McFarlane and Rutherford, 2008). This work highlights the central role of infrastructures in shaping urban food systems and conditioning their potential transformations and outcomes (LOGIC et al., 2024). However, the field remains emergent, with no settled conceptualisation of urban food infrastructures, ranging from studies focused on physical systems to broader assemblage approaches incorporating social dimensions (Middha, 2022; Myers & Caruso, 2016; Rut & Davies, 2024; Williams & Tait, 2023). Research on urban food infrastructures offers an opportunity to establish a dialogue across food and urban studies and to advance research that addresses urban food inequalities in their material, spatial, and socio-political dimensions.

In this panel, we use infrastructures as an entry point to establish a dialogue on cities' powers to transform socio-ecological systems and enact positive change, from health to climate change. The panel will draw on insights from different geographies, theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions to reflect on how urban food infrastructures and urban powers are conceptualised and mobilised as means to understand and transform cities and food.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Beyond Access: Lived Experience and Disability Justice in Tourism and Leisure Spaces (2)
schedule_event.description:
Tourism provides significant potential for well-being, social connection, and leisure engagement. However, these benefits are not equally distributed or accessible across the population. This session moves “beyond access” to centre the rich diversity of lived experience and the transformative framework of disability justice within tourism and leisure geographies. Directly addressing the conference theme, “Geographies of Inequality,” this session explores what it truly means to establish tourism and leisure spaces that are not simply compliant, but are genuinely welcoming, equitable, and inclusive of human diversity.

This session aims to bridge a connection between scholarship on embodiment and affect andcritical principles of disability justice. Drawing on this framework that demands not just entry, but also equitable participation, intersectional analysis, and collective liberation, we invite papers that prioritize the knowledge and experiences of disabled people to explore how leisure spaces are navigated, contested, and re-imagined. We are particularly interested in research that moves beyond a singular focus on physical impairment to embrace neurodiversity, sensory worlds, and the complex interplay of multiple social identities.

Potential topics include, but are not limited to:
• Critiques of checklist-style accessibility and the pursuit of belonging, dignity, and joy.
• The sensory, affective, and embodied geographies of navigating, negotiating, or avoiding tourist destinations.
• Intersectional analyses where disability meets race, gender, queerness, and class in leisure contexts.
• Disability activism, community-led tourism, and the co-design of just and inclusive spaces.
• Re-imagining heritage and nature-based tourism through a disability justice lens.
• The role of digital platforms and media in fostering justice.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Walking on Uneven Ground: Access, Belonging and Environmental Change on Trail Landscapes (1)
schedule_event.description:
Trail-based activities such as hiking, trail running and off-road cycling are often imagined as accessible and inclusive practices, particularly within rural landscapes that are framed as shared, equal and open spaces for all. Yet geographers and social scientists increasingly demonstrate that they are not socially or environmentally neutral. Rather, they are infrastructures through which uneven relations of power, belonging, and responsibility are organised (Cloke & Little, 1997; Neal, 2002; Chakraborti & Garland, 2004; Shrubsole, 2019; Fowler, 2020).

Across footpaths, rights of way, bridleways, access land, and National Trails, everyday practices of movement produce differentiated experiences of comfort, entitlement, surveillance, and exclusion along lines of race, class, gender, disability, age, and nationality. At the same time, trails are increasingly shaped by environmental pressures associated with climate change, biodiversity loss, and landscape degradation. These changes are often encountered not abstractly, but sensorially and affectively: through eroded paths, altered seasonal rhythms, flood-damaged routes, or transformed ecologies. As such, trails operate as sites where inequalities of access and belonging intersect with questions of care, responsibility, and human–environment relations.

Whilst outdoor recreation has been examined through mobilities (Snead et al., 2009) leisure (Lanclus, 2023), tourism (Timothy & Boyd, 2015), wellbeing (Pomfret et al., 2024) and heritage (Svensson et al., 2022), trails themselves remain comparatively under-theorised as spacial formations that structure both social relations and environmental engagements. Frequently marketed as universally welcoming and resilient spaces, trails are none-the-less shaped by legal frameworks, governance, historical narratives and affective atmospheres that privilege particular bodies, practices, and modes of environmental engagement while marginalising others.

This session brings together work that critically examines trails as uneven and unsettled landscapes of movement. The first timeslot will focus on issues of racialized belonging on trails. Elisa Sisto’s paper deals with the uneasy coexistence between histories of refuge and contemporary regimes of surveillance in the Alps and Pyrenees. Elsa Mertala’s contribution foregrounds lived experiences of Black hikers in the Scottish outdoors while Mohammed Dhalech looks at racialized belonging and care along the Hadrian’s Wall trail. The first timeslot concludes with Tom Bennett’s paper which interrogates how national trails in England, often framed as a rural idyll, are in fact entangled with wider geographies of deprivation. Taken together, these papers unsettle assumptions about who belongs on the trail, foregrounding the social, political and affective negotiations embedded in seemingly neutral landscapes.

paper.title: Commemorated and Criminalised:  Mountain Trails and the Politics of Belonging in the Alps and Pyrenees
Walking into belonging: Black joy in the Scottish outdoors
Walking Hadrian’s Wall trail: Spontaneous walking interviews, racialised belonging and care as connection with nature
Destabilising the Idyll: National Trails and the Geography of Deprivation

This session brings together work that critically examines trails as uneven and unsettled landscapes of movement. The second timeslot will focus on senses of place and embodied experiences on trails. Felicitas Kuebler’s paper delves into the limits of enjoyment and the negative atmospheres by approaching hiking in the Alps as embodied micro-practices. Dave McLaughlin explores the extent to which walking the International Appalachian Trail works to create space which holds across the transatlantic ‘Appalachian Divide’. By examining the trail-walking experiences at the Mogao Caves in Dunhuang, China, Chang Guo reveals how top-down heritage preservation influences perceptions of landscape and place. The second timeslot concludes with Eilis Lanclus’ paper which draws on sensory ethnographic work with trail sport participants in the UK and Belgium to unpack the duality between the growing desire of moving through ‘wild(er)’ landscapes and the ecological pressures these activities put on those landscapes. Taken together, these papers reveal how trails are lived and felt through the body, showing that movements through trail landscapes are inseparable from shifting senses of place and environmental change.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Climate justice, creativity and humour: learning with and from children and young people (1)
schedule_event.description:
Recent years have seen growing acknowledgement of the essential role of humour in communicating about climate change, including in education (Gerlofs and Zheng, 2025; Russell et al., 2023). This may seem at odds with such a serious topic. However, humour is a recognised tool of social justice (Borum Chattoo and Feldman, 2020) and may offer new and engaging approaches for climate change education (CCE) in the face of the climate fatigue and despair reported among many young people (Hickman et al., 2021; Sanson and Bellemo, 2021).

As scholars and practitioners conducting research with children and young people on climate-related topics, humour has become an intriguing aspect of our work. We are interested to hear from others who are similarly interested in the ways that fun, creativity, irony, irreverence and other comedic devices have and could become tools of climate justice, complementing or disrupting more traditional pedagogic methods.

We welcome traditional, creative or interactive presentations exploring children and young people’s own use of humour, their responses to others’ use of humour in CCE (or in wider climate communication), and especially research that involves children and young people in a participatory process, emphasizing co-production. The use of comedic devices in presentation delivery is warmly welcomed but not required!

Questions to be explored include, but are not limited to:

· Can there be fun in CCE? What role can humour play in CCE?

· Why is humour important when working in CCE?

· How can humour open serious questions of climate justice?

· How does humour enter CCE, through children and educators alike?

· How can or could humour work across language, culture and generational divides to support climate solidarity?

· How are humour and creativity mutually supporting?

· What are the ethics of using humour in research with children on climate change?

paper.title: Just Joking: Humour as Epistemic Practice in Climate Resilient Playground Co-Design
Playfulness, Humour, and Care in an Online Vietnamese Youth Climate Organisation
Children’s humour in their exploration of climate change and creation of eco-stories
Messing around with messy challenges
Creative and humorous forms of everyday resistance in the youth-led climate justice movement in the Philippines

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Subsumption (1): Social Reproduction and Urbanisation
schedule_event.description:
In recent work philosopher Andrés Saenz de Sicilia declares subsumption “the concept of capitalist domination…key to thinking the structure and development of capitalist societies” (2025: 115). As a conceptual tool able to parse the highly differential capture and command of labour, and wildly divergent appearance of exploitation in capitalist societies, subsumption has become invaluable to heterodox attempts to understand the volatile and crisis-ridden character of capitalism.

In opposition to theoretical approaches that conceptualise capitalism as a homologous and inert social artefact of the mid-19th century, as well as those who take the morbid and dysfunctional character of contemporary capitalism as a prompt to jump across the postcapitalist horizon, subsumption provides us with a resource to trace the anxious and contradictory character of capitalist power in the present conjuncture (Cowan 2026).

In this regard subsumption has been drawn upon by heterodox scholars in recent years to chart capital’s heterogeneity, informing an analytic of capitalist value able to account for diverse forms of capitalist exploitation, expropriation and coercion and capital’s untidy relation to social reproduction, racialisation and informality (Campbell, 2016; Chhabria, 2023; Clare 2019; Liu, 2022; Mezzadri, 2021; Tadiar, 2022). And yet despite the concept experiencing something of renaissance within heterodox theory more broadly, critical and marxist geographers (with some notable exceptions) have yet to fully engage with the spatial and geographical implications and characteristics of subsumption.

This session will explore the geographies of capitalist subsumption through an engagement with social reproduction and forces of urbanisation.

The concept of subsumption invites us to consider the sharply political grounds of capitalist settlement, and so we invite contributors too to consider how the terms of subsumption help shape both the emergence of new alliances and discords between capitalists and anticapitalists, and trace the fault-lines and leverage points of capitalist and anticapitalist power.

Subsuming Informality: Informal Landed Property and the Geographies of Differential Subsumption in Peripheral Brazil
Subsumption in Circulation: Platform Capital and Domestic Wage Labour in Urban India
Emigration Processing Zones: Migration Industries and the Social Reproduction of Labour in Punjab
Subsumed Landscapes: The Unruly ‘Flesh’ of Capitalist Urbanization

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Authoritarianism (1)
schedule_event.description:
State authoritarian practices are in the ascendency around the world and have been for several years (Diamond et al 2016). Geographers have an important role to play in understanding and mapping their development, analysing their causes and consequences, and identifying tactics that slow and reverse their progress (Koch 2022). The stakes are high: individual and collective freedoms are being eroded in economic, social, cultural, institutional, political, digital, intellectual, spiritual, reproductive and corporeal spaces (see Regilme 2021).

This session features papers that use geographical approaches and insights to speak to the philosophy, epistemology, and empirical nature of authoritarian practices. Philosophically, we need to understand how human freedom has become devalued - there is a need to comprehend the modes of thought that make authoritarianism a seductive state strategy. Epistemologically, we need to confront the reality that authoritarianism is hard, and sometimes dangerous, to study owing to the vested interest of states in concealing it – perspectives that establish principles for the safe and effective study of authoritarianism in the current era of increasingly constrained academic freedom are needed. Empirically, we need approaches that connect and traverse insights from the geographical sub-disciplines in recognition of the cross-cutting nature of authoritarian practices. We also need to reflect on the balance between defense and critique of ostensibly liberal practices and institutions that claim to seek to uphold freedom and human rights.

Whilst evidence and argument points towards the generalized pattern of increasing authoritarianism at a global scale (Diamond et al 2016) – a sense in which a similar set of authoritarian discourse, practice, and rule is taking place across the globe – it remains true that these authoritarianisms look and feel different in different places (Dahlmann 2022). There is not just one kind of authoritarianism which operates in the same way in different places. What then, can a distinctly geographical approach to authoritarianism contribute?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Authoritarianism (2)
schedule_event.description:
State authoritarian practices are in the ascendency around the world and have been for several years (Diamond et al 2016). Geographers have an important role to play in understanding and mapping their development, analysing their causes and consequences, and identifying tactics that slow and reverse their progress (Koch 2022). The stakes are high: individual and collective freedoms are being eroded in economic, social, cultural, institutional, political, digital, intellectual, spiritual, reproductive and corporeal spaces (see Regilme 2021).

This session features papers that use geographical approaches and insights to speak to the philosophy, epistemology, and empirical nature of authoritarian practices. Philosophically, we need to understand how human freedom has become devalued - there is a need to comprehend the modes of thought that make authoritarianism a seductive state strategy. Epistemologically, we need to confront the reality that authoritarianism is hard, and sometimes dangerous, to study owing to the vested interest of states in concealing it – perspectives that establish principles for the safe and effective study of authoritarianism in the current era of increasingly constrained academic freedom are needed. Empirically, we need approaches that connect and traverse insights from the geographical sub-disciplines in recognition of the cross-cutting nature of authoritarian practices. We also need to reflect on the balance between defense and critique of ostensibly liberal practices and institutions that claim to seek to uphold freedom and human rights.

Whilst evidence and argument points towards the generalized pattern of increasing authoritarianism at a global scale (Diamond et al 2016) – a sense in which a similar set of authoritarian discourse, practice, and rule is taking place across the globe – it remains true that these authoritarianisms look and feel different in different places (Dahlmann 2022). There is not just one kind of authoritarianism which operates in the same way in different places. What then, can a distinctly geographical approach to authoritarianism contribute?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Actually Existing ‘Green Transformations’: Between Straightforward Plans and the Messy Realities of Everyday Processes (1)
schedule_event.description:
While ‘transition’ is an alluring term promising a more environmentally conscious future, the ‘green transition’ has faced significant hurdles in recent years. Although climate change remains a pressing global crisis, international solidarity is fraying and sustainable policies are under threat in many regions. Consequently, the ‘actually experienced’ green transition has become far less certain than the linear change of conditions the term ‘transition’ proclaims.

In this sense, the green transition mirrors other major historical shifts. For instance, following the fall of the Berlin Wall, the ‘transitologist’ discourse of the 1990s anticipated a straightforward shift from state-centred systems toward democratic, market-driven societies. However, the lived experience of post-socialism was considerably less clear-cut. Striking analogies exist between these two narratives: both are defined as large-scale, disruptive changeovers (Healy & Barry, 2017) promising a new reality—whether integration into a Western capitalist future or a sustainable, livable one.

Regarding the 1990s, anthropologists and geographers highlighted the fragmentations and hybridizations of supposedly ‘clear-cut’ shifts, which proved much messier than political elites anticipated (Verdery, 1996; Stark & Bruszt, 1998; Pickles & Unwin, 2003; Sýkora & Bouzarovski, 2012; Hann, 2012). Similar observations are increasingly valid for contemporary green transitions (White, 2025). Exploring ‘actually existing’ transformations is necessary to uncover the multiplicity of simultaneous changes and the injustices inherent in these processes. For example, introducing technological fixes without revising consumption practices may exacerbate inequalities or rely on carbon-intensive extraction (Skrzypek et al., 2022). Ultimately, a holistic ecological transition may require moving beyond the capitalist imperative of growth toward a degrowth logic prioritizing ‘care, solidarity, mutual aid, and empathy’ (Buch-Hansen et al, 2024).

Much as in the story of post-socialism, a heuristic shift from transition to transformation offers a more nuanced insight into sustainability. While ‘transition’ often implies a managed shift between known states, ‘transformation’ suggests open-ended, turbulent, and conflicting processes occurring simultaneously in different directions.

This session views the ‘green transition’ as a complex societal transformation and invites scholars to reflect on the conflicts and contradictions of these processes as they emerge in practice. We aim to explore ‘messy realities’ through critical readings of contemporary changes, drawing inspiration from historical transformative processes across the Global North, South, and East.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Accounting for nature, or holding nature to account? Critical geographies of environmental accounting (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session stages a critical exploration of the links between geography, and cognate disciplines, and questions of environmental accounting. Over the last two decades, the environment has not only been increasingly taken into account - with its social and economic benefits measured, quantified, valued and priced - but also, more recently, held to account. While the former is often presented as a route to environmental improvement, it warrants scrutiny: environmental accounting is invariably partial, shaped by power relations, and uneven in its effects. The latter development is more troubling. As nature is ledgered and administered like any other asset, it becomes fungible, replaceable even, through accounting logics. This session will explore the Janus-faced nature of environmental accounting, where, on the one hand, accounting practices enable regimes of governance and place nature on the public balance sheet, on the other, they serve to demand a justification for nature’s existence via economic reasoning.

Carbon accounting brought something as nebulous as entities’ relations with the atmosphere onto the books, to assume (selective) carbon accountability, benchmark and rank progress, enable carbon markets and offsetting practices, and forecast decarbonisation pathways of benefit or minimal detriment to ‘business as usual’. Similarly, nature accounts, forming the basis of natural capital, biodiversity net gain, and the restoration economy, have further extended ‘stock-taking’ financial logics into the environmental domain. Governments, business, NGOs, and universities have largely acquiesced to the need for cost-benefit analyses in environmental decision-making, to the common sense of holding nature itself to account. The influence of economists, accountants, financial consultants and investors has significantly grown as a result, challenging that of ecologists and conservation bodies, at the fuzzy intersection of science, politics, and economics.

Beyond professionals, everyone can provide their own account of ‘the environment’. But amidst its popular conflation with economic accounting, it is often reserved as a practice for experts and technocratic management, delimiting who gets to speak for the environment. In response, geographers, STS scholars, and others have sought to foreground the potential of overlooked perspectives, including radical, decolonial, artistic, and activist backgrounds. This session invites contributions to contemplate and discuss the threats, limits and affordances of balance sheet nature.

paper.title: Holding rivers to account
AI for SEEA: Practices and technologies of natural capital accounting
The Outsized Influence of Big Tech on Metrics, Standards, and Benchmarks in Carbon Accounting
Insetting the Record Straight: Environmental credits, commensuration, and the politics of carbon accounting

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Children's Legal Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description: Navigating legal landscapes can be challenging for young people as they work out where they belong and what spaces they have access to. This ‘working out’  involves intentional and unintentional transgression and an ongoing questioning of legal boundaries that can challenge parents, teachers, strangers, lawmakers and property owners to reflect on the taken-for-grantedness of law. It can also create openings to refigure legalised socio-spatial relations, especially as young people increasingly become involved in legal advocacy. This first of two paper sessions explores what centering young people’s perspectives about and experiences with the law can tell us about how we develop and normalise hegemonic legal geographies, and the possibilities of imagining and enacting alternatives. In doing so, it aims to develop discussion around children’s legal geographies (Noterman and Blomley 2024) – or how young people encounter, understand, and participate in law-making and breaking through their everyday spatial practices and imaginaries.

paper.title: Where are the children in the lawscape? A discussion on children's visibility in the urban space
Between hegemony and advocacy: Researching children’s right to a legal identity
Sonic rights for homeless neurodivergent children living in temporary accommodation
Learning property: navigating children's legal geographies of 'real' estate

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Play, Learn, Navigate: Exploring the role of serious games for a sustainable Ocean (1)
schedule_event.description:
Serious games, designed for educational purposes rather than just for entertainment, have long been used in geography curriculum from primary school to higher education (Robinson et al., 2021), in leadership training and for engaging policymakers (Sabherwal et al., 2024). They are increasingly recognised as powerful tools to enhance ocean literacy and promote environmental education related to sustainable ocean management, both within and outside of formal educational systems (e.g. engaging stakeholders or conducting research) (e.g. FishBanks, GoJelly, Coastcraft). These serious games encompass various formats, including tabletop games, screen-based games (such as online games or apps), and role-playing games (RPGs). Such games allow players to gain a better understanding of complex issues and experiment with different choices, encouraging critical, long-term and holistic thinking (Carr, 2025). They can also be essential for public engagement and informing decision-making. However, navigating these resources can be challenging, and it is often unclear how to measure their success, i.e. their influence on desired behavioural change, and the knowledge-action gap.

This session will explore empirical, methodological, and conceptual contributions that focus on the use of games and simulations to promote ocean literacy and support education on sustainable ocean and coastal development and management, including, but not limited to, questions, such as:

  • How can serious games be tailored to address issues of equity and justice in environmental education?
  • How do different formats of serious games impact player engagement and learning outcomes?
  • What metrics can be used to measure the effectiveness of serious games in promoting ocean literacy and sustainable ocean management?

This workshop will not only include presentations but will also offer participants the opportunity to immerse themselves in a range of serious games and reflect on the connections between theory and practice. The session will explore the design principles of serious games that engage people in interacting with complex systems behaviours.

paper.title: Shall we play at marine spatial planning? Experience and insights from the MSP Challenge board game

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Human Presence Beyond Earth: Critical Geographies of Outer Space (1)
schedule_event.description:
After 54 years, humans are returning to extraterrestrial environments, namely around and on the Moon. Unlike the Apollo missions, which focused on exploration and return, today's lunar programs envision permanent settlement—transforming the Moon from a destination for brief visits into a new human territory. In February 2026, for instance, NASA plans to launch Artemis II, which will send four humans beyond Earth’s orbit for the first time since 1972 as they slingshot around the moon. As humans prepare to return to Earth’s only natural satellite, how can geographers critically engage with this development while challenging uneven power relations? Building on geographers' long-standing inquiry into outer space and recent publications debating human space activities (Armstrong & Klinger, 2025; Bennett and Cudney, 2025; Klinger, 2021; Squire et al., 2021), this session continues this line of investigation by exploring critical geographies of outer space.

Contemporary space exploration differs fundamentally from the Apollo era, unfolding within a broader transformation of global geopolitics (Daniels & Krige, 2022) and commercialization (Denis et al., 2020). The bipolar order of the Cold War has given way to a multipolar landscape structured by technological rivalry, particularly between the United States and China, shaping these countries' space imaginations and missions (Puente-Lozano & Herring-Bazo, 2024; Wijeyeratne, 2026). Space exploration is no longer dominated by nation-states alone, but increasingly shaped by commercial–state partnerships, private capital, and hybrid governance arrangements emerging from shifting geopolitical configurations (Utrata, 2024). Proposals for "Moon villages," permanent infrastructures, and energy systems—including nuclear power and data centers—signal a move from exploration to settlement, raising urgent questions about sovereignty, territorialization, and control beyond Earth (Au, 2024; Vermeylen & Njeri, 2023).

Earth’s orbital space is becoming increasingly crowded and integrated into social, political, and economic dynamics both on Earth and beyond (Bowen, 2020; Dunnett et al., 2019). Critical scholars have questioned who is accessing outer space and how these space missions are enabled through uneven, racialized, and even colonial exploitation on Earth (Armstrong & Klinger, 2025; Klinger et al., 2025; Redfield, 2000). Research has also explored how human perception and sensory experience shape engagements with outer space, as scholars develop and reflect on research methods for studying space and space-related practices (Praet & Pitrou, 2025; Timko et al., 2022; Vertesi, 2015). Outer space is not simply another research project: it is interwoven with terrestrial dynamics through the spacecraft, infrastructure, imagination, and legal systems that connect humanity to space (Messeri & Olson, 2025). As outer space assumes greater importance in geopolitical power and geo-economic competition, questions arise about how space infrastructure and its entanglement with Earth's social, cultural, political, and colonial histories reproduce existing uneven power relations beyond our planet.

As outer space becomes increasingly entangled with Earth-based political economies, geographers are well positioned to interrogate this spatial reordering. This session examines how outer space is grounded in terrestrial infrastructures, extractive economies, and geopolitical power, revealing that the space frontier is deeply embedded in Earthly histories and inequalities.

Grounding Heaven: The Luigi Broglio Space Centre and the Geopolitics of Space Infrastructure

Meteorites and the Making of a Terrestrial Space Economy
Space Rock Materialities: Deep Pasts, Extractive Presents and Speculative Futures

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Reproductive Justice: Inequalities in Becoming and Being Parents (1) - Structures of Inequality
schedule_event.description:
Parenting unfolds amid intersecting social, legal, political, biological, and economic inequalities across diverse spaces, encompassing the making and breaking of families (Smietana, Thompson, and Twine 2018). This panel interrogates the geographies of reproductive—defined as a) the right to have children, b) the right not to have children, c) the right to raise children amid supportive circumstances (Ross and Solinger 2017), and the right to sexual autonomy (Ross 2021). Specifically, we are interested in how people become and live as parents —or fail to do so—amid relational and structural inequities within the "global intimate space of reproduction" (Schurr et al. 2025, 10).

We invite contributions critically examining the “inequalities of becoming and being parents” among marginalised groups across intersectional axes, transcending simplistic dichotomies of invisibilisation/stigmatisation versus happiness/joy. We seek empirically grounded, conceptually rich papers illuminating the lived realities of (intended) parents’ fluid identities and shifting family arrangements in diverse settings. Contributions from the Majority World highlighting unequal experiences—along dimensions of race, ethnicity, indigeneity, class, gender, sexuality, disability, chronic illness, infertility, neurodiversity, religion, or citizenship—are especially welcome. Possible themes include (but are not limited to):

  • (Im)possibilities of parenting amid crises (e.g., poverty, climate change, war),
  • Family separations (e.g., due to prisons, borders, militarized zones) and transnational parenting
  • Queer reproductive justice
  • Social parents and chosen families
  • Access barriers to parenthood/parenting support for disabled individuals
  • Everyday challenges of neurodivergent parents
  • Migrantised and racialised experiences

We encourage case studies, comparative analyses, and methodological reflections that critically engage with reproduction and parenting in diverse contexts. We expect 15-minute in-person presentations, with ample time for discussion.

References

Ross, Loretta. 2021. "Reproductive Justice. Ein Rahmen für eine anti-essentialistische und intersektionale Politik." In Mehr als Selbstbestimmung! Kämpfe für reproduktive Gerechtigkeit, edited by Kitchen Politics, 17-60. Münster: edition Assemblage.

Ross, Loretta, and Rickie Solinger. 2017. Reproductive justice: An introduction. Oakland: University of California Press.

Schurr, Carolin, Susanne Schultz, Nora Komposch, Nina Etter, Laura Perler, and Yolinliztli Pérez-Hérnandez. 2025. "Geographies of reproductive justice." Progress in Human Geography 0 (0):1-21. doi: 10.1177/03091325251338823.

Smietana, Marcin, Charis Thompson, and France Winddance Twine. 2018. "Making and breaking families–reading queer reproductions, stratified reproduction and reproductive justice together." Reproductive Biomedicine & Society Online 7:112-130.

Reproductive Justice and the Politics of Prenatal Optimisation: Holistic Fertility Programmes in India
Social Disparities in Reproductive Self-Determination in Switzerland

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - No Space Like Home? Space inequalities, property development and urban injustice (1)
schedule_event.description:
Organised by Ruth Neville (CASA, UCL), Katherine Brickell (Geography, KCL); Helen Carr (Law, University of Southampton); Ella Harris (Geography, KCL); Phil Hubbard (Geography, KCL), Iliana Ortega-Alcazar (Geography & Planning, University of Sheffield); Jon Reades (CASA, UCL) and Eleanor Wilkinson (Geography & Planning, University of Sheffield).

Housing systems around the world are being reshaped by affordability pressures, demographic changes, and shifting policies, with many of the latter favouring densification and the delivery of new housing at pace. While debates around housing often focus on the rate and price at which additional housing units are provided, less attention has been paid to the unequal distribution of housing space itself. This means we lack understanding of how much domestic space different social groups and populations have access to, where this is located, and how this enhances, or diminishes their quality of life. This is despite the consensus that the dimensionality of housing helps determine whether it functions not simply as a shelter, but as a space that feels like home.
The session aims to advance understandings of how housing space is produced, valued, contested, and lived, and how it might be governed or redistributed in support of more just and liveable housing futures.

We particularly welcome contributions that explore, amongst others, the following:
⚡ The measurement, mapping or modelling of inequalities in housing space, quality, and affordability using different data sources
⚡ The use of qualitative, creative or sensory methods to explore the affective, emotional and relational dimensions of domestic space
⚡ Health discrimination and the well-being impacts of social overcrowding and space insufficiency
⚡ The environmental and social sustainability of different allocations and distributions of housing space
⚡ The role of planning, policy, and regulatory frameworks in encouraging equality of access to housing space
⚡ The significance of prop-tech, platform economies and finance in the changing distribution of housing space

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Lost in Post-Fossil Transformations
schedule_event.description:
Socio-ecological transformations towards more sustainable, post-fossil, and just societies currently unfold through contentious and ongoing negotiations. Transformation is never only about turning towards something new; it also entails turning away from established practices, infrastructures, and ways of life. The escalating climate crisis and associated phase-outs and policy shifts foreground processes of disappearance, in which certain domains must be dismantled or discontinued. In this sense, transformation produces experiential forms of loss (Reckwitz 2024) and creates the conditions for emerging societal modes of farewell, letting go, or dealing with “transition pain” (Bogner et al. 2024). Developing further debates on emotional dimensions of sustainability transitions, this session focuses on how practices, affects, politics, and spaces of loss are implicated in transformative contexts, shaping socio-political relations and future pathways.

Loss has been discussed in geography, for example in relation to ecological loss and damage in a climate-changed world (Cunsolo & Landmann 2017; Hepach & Hertz 2023), as well as in studies of deindustrialisation, ruination, and vacancy (Meier 2012; Rohse et al. 2020; Zhang 2024). Only very recently have the first explicit attempts been formulated to conceptualize the “geographies of loss” (Runkel & Schramm 2024). To delimit an otherwise broad field, the session focuses on current socio-ecological transformations, including sustainability transitions, climate policies, and shifts away from fossil-based ways of being. At the same time, it approaches transformation more broadly as a situated and lived experience, and therefore resists tying transformation-related losses too narrowly to fixed objects or dominant narratives such as deindustrialisation or ecological collapse. Instead, the session foregrounds loss as dispersed, unevenly materialising, and socially differentiated, emerging within wider societal shifts and power relations. Contributions may explore how loss accompanies transformation from the outset—anticipated when change is envisioned or engineered, reactivated through past experiences, or expressed as mourning for futures that never materialised.

Against the backdrop of dominant narratives of progress, spaces for collective engagement with loss are often limited (Elliott 2018; Reckwitz 2024). This session therefore asks how practices and spaces of loss might enable social participation in transformation, and whether engaging with loss can open ethical and political perspectives for shaping just transitions. Loss raises difficult questions about the legitimacy of holding on and about how politically problematic sentiments—such as claims that “things were better before”—can be addressed without slipping into nostalgic or reactionary positions, or arguing about the moral order of societal ‘losers’ and what legitimacy groups have for trying to “hold on”. Yet if loss can function as a corrective to one-sided logics of progress, or as a critique of sustainability’s tendency toward preservation (rather than toward exploring progressive forms of letting go; Elliott 2018), we invite contributions that explore how the perspective of loss might be mobilised in critical and progressive ways.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Sustaining Cultural Tourism Places: Heritage Value, Livelihoods, and Long-Term Governance
schedule_event.description:
Across diverse regions and institutional settings, the coupling of culture and tourism is reshaping local development trajectories. From historic cities in Europe, to cultural tourism towns in China, and tourism villages and community destinations in Southeast Asia, heritage resources, cultural practices, and visitor economies are increasingly mobilised through place branding, regeneration, and wider processes of economic restructuring. Yet sustainability in these places rarely emerges as an automatic outcome. More often, it is produced through ongoing negotiations and trade-offs among growth, conservation, liveability, and environmental limits, negotiations that may reproduce as well as challenge existing geographical inequalities. Tourism expansion can generate income and employment while intensifying housing pressures, crowding public space, and producing precarious and seasonal labour that disproportionately affects marginalised groups. Heritage protection can support cultural continuity and local identity, but it can also sharpen tensions around commodification and contested notions of authenticity. Meanwhile, infrastructure capacity, ecological constraints, risk governance, and climate adaptation raise further questions about what can be sustained, by whom, and at what cost.

This session brings together scholars and practitioners working on cultural tourism places and welcomes theoretical and empirical contributions that engage with cultural, social, economic, and environmental dimensions of sustainability across contexts. We particularly encourage work that advances international and interdisciplinary dialogue and methodological innovation, including:

      • Ethnography and in-depth interviews

      • Policy and institutional analysis

      • Spatial, digital, and data-driven approaches

      • Participatory evaluation and community-based methods

      • Sustainability assessment frameworks and indicator systems

Possible themes include, but are not limited to:

      • Benefit distribution and local livelihoods

      • Resident wellbeing, social justice, and everyday liveability

      • Heritage authorisation, cultural participation, and authenticity politics

      • Overtourism, regeneration, and rural or urban transformation

      • Platformisation, tourism work, and labour precarity

      • Carrying capacity, resource governance, and environmental pressures

      • Crisis resilience and climate adaptation

      • Long-term maintenance, public service provision, and institutionalised governance beyond project cycles

The session aims to create a cross-regional conversation around a shared question: in culture- and tourism-led development, what is being sustained, for whom, through which mechanisms, and how can places build workable long-term balances between growth, heritage, and everyday life?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Infrastructural Deaths (1): The Geographies and Temporalities of Infrastructural Deaths
schedule_event.description:
“[T]here is no ‘end’ to infrastructure”, state Ramakrishnan et al. (2021: 682) in their call for researchers to move beyond temporal assumptions of infrastructures that transition linearly from creation to afterlife and focus instead on ongoing transformations in infrastructural relations. However, recent events such as the political crisis around the irreversible shut down of the UK’s Scunthorpe steelworks also reveal that infrastructural ‘deaths’ – real or perceived – can have extraordinary political salience and should not be ignored.

To date, infrastructural deaths have been examined in a limited way in the interdisciplinary literature on infrastructural politics. Research has explored infrastructural disruptions and failures (Graham, 2010; Ranganathan, 2016; Silver, 2016), decay and ruination (Gupta, 2018; Ramakrishnan et al. 2021; Silver, 2021), infrastructural afterlives (Evans, 2021; Morris, 2021; Pike, 2019), urbicide and the strategic military ‘killing’ of infrastructure (Coward, 2008; Graham, 2005), the effects of infrastructural absences/disconnections (Kinder, 2016; Young & Keil, 2010), the (death) tolls of infrastructural labour (Addie, 2021; Tyner, 2019), and the decline of infrastructures through deindustrialisation (Emery, 2019; Pulido, 2016; Zhang, 2024). Surprisingly little social research has examined infrastructural decommissioning and retirement. What does exist has emerged in other fields such as energy geographies (De Laurentis & Windemere, 2024; McCauley, 2018; Windemere, 2019), engineering (Invernizzi et al. 2020), and law (Dike, 2017).

Within this work, deaths have often been described in singular forms (urbicide), have been presented implicitly as temporary conditions (disruptions and failures), or have already happened (afterlives). In other cases, the political significance of death as a total, irrevocable, or inevitable phase state has been overlooked (decay/ruination), or the uniqueness of infrastructure as an object of analysis has been sidelined (deindustrialisation). Further research is therefore needed to conceptualise the political salience of infrastructural deaths, their geographies and temporalities, and the ways in which infrastructural constellations become reconfigured in relation to infrastructural deaths.

This session presents reflections on:

  • What it means for an infrastructure to ‘die’ and the forms deaths take
  • Where infrastructural deaths happen, over what timescales
  • How infrastructural deaths are imagined, anticipated, contested, and managed, and the ways in which they recast the political relations surrounding infrastructures
  • The labour required to anticipate, facilitate, mitigate, or prevent infrastructural deaths
  • (Uneven) experiences of infrastructural deaths and the inequalities that produce/are reproduced through them
  • The conditions of possibility infrastructural deaths create for new forms of social justice

paper.title: German Lignite Coal Infrastructure Between Burial and Exhumation; Death and Resurrection.
Conceptualising Infrastructural Deaths

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Beyond local conditions: conceptualising ‘place’ for local climate action
schedule_event.description:
Geographers have been at the forefront of research into local responses to climate change for over two decades. Recent geographical work on local conditions for local climate policy, local transformative capacities, the role of small enterprises or communities and social movement activism, all allude to the importance of local factors, networks and processes in the development and implementation of local climate policies. Surprisingly, however, the notion of ‘place’ is rarely foregrounded in these studies, producing a crucial omission taken the importance of ‘place’ as one core concept in human geography debates.

Therefore, the aim of this session is to strengthen the conception of ‘place’ in work on local climate action, and to establish a dialogue with classical conceptions of place within human geography. Works of John Agnew, Doreen Massey, Anssi Paasi and Tim Creswell, to name a few, put forward a relational understanding of place, targeting the dynamics how places are becoming constructed, and contested, through different layers of social relations. Importantly, this implies moving beyond conceptions of place as fixed and solely local, stressing the embeddedness of place and place-making processes in developments across locations and scales.

Speaking to different contexts, this session seeks to clarify how (1) given material and political structures, (2) emotive underpinnings and (3) agency and power dynamics serve or impede the development of capacities for climate action; and how place features are, in turn, reshaped in collaborations and conflicts around tackling climate change, including struggles over what constitutes a “just place” in a climate action context. We contend that by unearthing the dynamics around inclusion and exclusion, and accounting for linkages across places, a critical perspective on place will open new pathways for conceptualising issues of justice in local climate action and beyond.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Imaginations of Just Transitions – Visualizations of (in)justice, politics and (im)mobility in socio-ecological transformations
schedule_event.description:
Climate change can hardly be perceived from a purely scientific perspective only, as it has deep-seated consequences for how our societies are organized socially, economically, and culturally (Horn, 2024). This has been recognized within multiple studies that focus on the way climate change is visualized on social media, in film and series, in photography, and in the visual arts. In times when we often no longer have the language to describe the changes we are facing, these visual representations of change are becoming increasingly powerful as they can help us to understand the materiality of climate change (Rusca, 2024).

In this session, we are keen to explore what role visual artefacts (tangible or digital) play within the perceptions and imaginations of possible socio-ecological transformations needed to address climate change. Geographers criticize the frequent use of apocalyptic scenarios and images that convey uncertainty (Beuret, 2024). They also criticize the stoking of fears, such as the fear of climate migration, and the externalization of the vulnerabilities and consequences of climate change to the Global South (Manzo, 2020; Johnson, 2011). 

Durand-Delacre (2022) therefore argues that it is time to find new visualisations and imaginations if we do not want to run the risk of shutting down alternatives for just socio-ecological transformations. Rusca (2024) argues similarly, showing that we no longer need simple disaster scenarios to alert people to the looming disaster of climate change, but rather politically nuanced visions of the future that address issues of power and inequality to achieve just transformations. This also includes asking who is given space to develop new imaginations and what forms of knowledge are taken into account (Braun, 2015; Harris, 2022). Possible questions for the session thus include:

How is climate change adaptation and transformation depicted and interpreted visually?

How are issues such as (in)justice and (im)mobility addressed within such visual representations?

Which motives dominate the visual discourse of climate change adaptation and transformation, and what effect do they have?

How can these representations be broken down and critically questioned? Which methods are suitable for this?

How can geographers support the development of critical imaginations and visualisations of just socio-ecological transformations in the context of climate change?

Literature:

Beuret, N. (2024). Mapping the catastrophic imaginary: The organisation of environmental politics through climate change. In: Environment and Planning E: Nature and Space 7 (1), S. 252–270. DOI: 10.1177/25148486231168395.

Braun, B. (2015). Futures: Imagining Socioecological Transformation—An Introduction. Annals of the Association of American Geographers, 105(2), 239-243.

Durand-Delacre, D. (2022). (Mis)representing climate mobilities: lessons from documentary filmmaking. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 48(14), 3397-3415.

Harris, D. M. (2021). Storying climate knowledge: Notes on experimental political ecology. Geoforum, 126, 331-339.

Horn, E. (2024). Klima. Eine Wahrnehmungsgeschichte. Fischer.

Johnson, H. L. (2011). Click to Donate: visual images, constructing victims and imagining the female refugee. Third World Quarterly, 32(6), 1015-1037.

Manzo, K. (2010). Imaging vulnerability: the iconography of climate change. Area, 42(1), 96-107.

Rusca, M. (2024). Towards a future-oriented political ecology of climate change. Geoforum, 153, 103921.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The ‘unlikely’ geography of climate leadership and innovation (1): Anticipatory governance
schedule_event.description:
Place-based climate leadership is usually associated with economically successful cities and regions, which have strong institutional capacity, fiscal resources, and favourable structural conditions (e.g. van der Heijden, 2019). In contrast, economically disadvantaged, peripheral and non-metropolitan places are often cast as vulnerable territories that are further peripheralised by net-zero, energy and green transitions (e.g. Golubchikov and O’Sullivan, 2020). This aligns with economic and political geography scholarship that emphasises the political constraints and institutional deficits faced by ‘left-behind’ places (e.g. Rodríguez-Pose, 2018). However, evidence also emerges that some of the most economically disadvantaged places become national leaders in climate and net-zero policy and governance (Golubchikov et al., 2026). Undoubtedly, economic agglomeration and favourable structural conditions matter, but they are not deterministic (Haupt et al., 2023). Motivated local actors – often working through coalitions spanning local government, civil society and businesses – can shape strong climate vision and action (Homsy, 2018; Haupt et al., 2025). The lesson is that climate and net-zero leadership is a story of a more distributed geography than is often assumed.

Understanding this ‘unlikely geography’ of climate leadership remains limited, yet it is important for ensuring a genuinely just transition. First, recognising leadership and innovation beyond economically successful regions is essential for epistemic justice: it challenges who is seen as a legitimate source of innovation, governance and experimentation. Second, the experiences of less wealthy and peripheral places are potentially more transferable to the other regions facing similar constraints than the more celebrated cases. Two further key issues, however, are economic impact and political durability. Can such ‘peripheral’ climate leadership translate into wider economic and social opportunities for these places? And can it be sustained over time amid persistent institutional and financial limitations, alongside intensifying backlash and contestation around climate policy?

This session includes contributions that investigate this ‘unlikely’ geography of climate leadership – exploring how poorer and peripheral places are not only sites of risk and vulnerability, but also potential innovators, leaders, and agenda-setters in the net-zero transition.

schedule_event.description:
Place-based climate leadership is usually associated with economically successful cities and regions, which have strong institutional capacity, fiscal resources, and favourable structural conditions (e.g. van der Heijden, 2019). In contrast, economically disadvantaged, peripheral and non-metropolitan places are often cast as vulnerable territories that are further peripheralised by net-zero, energy and green transitions (e.g. Golubchikov and O’Sullivan, 2020). This aligns with economic and political geography scholarship that emphasises the political constraints and institutional deficits faced by ‘left-behind’ places (e.g. Rodríguez-Pose, 2018). However, evidence also emerges that some of the most economically disadvantaged places become national leaders in climate and net-zero policy and governance (Golubchikov et al., 2026). Undoubtedly, economic agglomeration and favourable structural conditions matter, but they are not deterministic (Haupt et al., 2023). Motivated local actors – often working through coalitions spanning local government, civil society and businesses – can shape strong climate vision and action (Homsy, 2018; Haupt et al., 2025). The lesson is that climate and net-zero leadership is a story of a more distributed geography than is often assumed.

Understanding this ‘unlikely geography’ of climate leadership remains limited, yet it is important for ensuring a genuinely just transition. First, recognising leadership and innovation beyond economically successful regions is essential for epistemic justice: it challenges who is seen as a legitimate source of innovation, governance and experimentation. Second, the experiences of less wealthy and peripheral places are potentially more transferable to the other regions facing similar constraints than the more celebrated cases. Two further key issues, however, are economic impact and political durability. Can such ‘peripheral’ climate leadership translate into wider economic and social opportunities for these places? And can it be sustained over time amid persistent institutional and financial limitations, alongside intensifying backlash and contestation around climate policy?

This session includes contributions that investigate this ‘unlikely’ geography of climate leadership – exploring how poorer and peripheral places are not only sites of risk and vulnerability, but also potential innovators, leaders, and agenda-setters in the net-zero transition.

paper.title: Engaging ‘unlikely’ participants in hands-on climate action to develop more inclusive climate policy?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of claims-making in international law (1)
schedule_event.description:
There is near-universal agreement—in policy circles, academic debate, and public discourse—that international law is undergoing a moment of profound crisis. Whether through the erosion of long-standing multilateral institutions, escalating geopolitical fragmentation, or disputes over the legitimacy of international legal norms, commentators frequently diagnose a system under strain, if not experiencing outright rupture (e.g. Kinstler 2025; Tzouvala 2025). Yet paradoxically, this crisis has not diminished claims on and to international law. If anything, the actors who invoke, contest, and rework international law have multiplied. An increasingly diverse array of participants including states, social movements, Indigenous Peoples, corporations, NGOs and transnational advocacy networks turn to international legality—sometimes strategically and sometimes aspirationally—as a register through which to assert political claims, demand accountability, and imagine alternative political futures. The contemporary landscape is therefore marked by an expansion, rather than a retreat, of claims-making in international law. This expansion, in turn, raises important questions for geographers. Where, how, and by whom is international law being articulated, negotiated, and spatialised? With what social, material, and political effects? And how should these phenomena be reconciled with the challenges the international legal order currently faces?

This session brings together scholars to examine the geographies of international legal claims-making. In doing so, we speak to the relative neglect of international law within legal geography scholarship (Jones and Smith 2015; Delaney 2017), while also encouraging greater dialogue between legal, political, and historical geographers (McGeachan 2017; Braverman et al. 2014; Graham and Bartel 2016).

We invite contributions engaging with (but not limited to) the following questions:

  • What geographical imaginaries underpin and are produced through practices of international legal claims-making?
  • How do sites such as courts and treaty bodies, in addition to ‘alternative’ sites of legal mobilisation, shape the forms and possibilities of international legal claims-making?
  • What spatial practices (e.g. mapping, documenting, witnessing, archiving) underpin the construction of international legal claims?
  • How do international legal claims circulate across local, national, regional, and global scales, and how are they transformed in the process?
  • What material infrastructures support or constrain international legal claims-making?
  • How does engaging in international legal claims-making contribute to the formation or transformation of geopolitical subjectivities?
  • How do practices of international legal claims-making interact with, reinforce, or challenge domestic legal regimes?
  • How can attention to historical forms of international legal claims-making illuminate the dynamics of the present global conjuncture?
  • What role can geographers and geographical knowledge play in the construction of international legal claims?
  • What methods are best suited to the study of international legal claims-making

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Coming Into View: Emerging Voices and Expanding Horizons in Animal Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
Animal geography has developed into a vibrant and diverse field of inquiry at the frontiers of critical geographical scholarship. Researchers continue to advance efforts to decolonise and globalise animal geographies (Hovorka 2017), refine ethical and political engagements with animal life and its study (Gibbs, 2024), and push the subdiscipline’s limits through attention to uncharismatic, unconventional and previously overlooked nonhuman animals (Pille-Schneider, 2024; Philip, 2024; Oliver and Dickinson, 2024). Important work nonetheless remains for animal geographers, as they bring to fore new regions, relations, knowledges, and critters that have so far been relatively underexplored in the field (Philo et al., 2024).

We seek to showcase the work of emerging scholars – postgraduate and early career researchers – who are forging new empirical, conceptual, and methodological paths forward for animal geographies. In light of this year’s conference focus on geographies of (in)equalities, we especially welcome contributions that explore how more just places for animals might be realised, as well as how animal geography can further do justice to the ethical, political, and epistemic promises of its agenda.

Alongside conventional paper presentations, we encourage works that use exploratory, arts-based, and experimental ways of presenting research.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Inequalities from Space
schedule_event.description:
This inclusive, hands-on workshop introduces participants to data products derived from satellite imagery and demonstrates how they can be used to better understand social and environmental inequalities. At the Imago: Data Service for Imagery, we transform complex earth observation data into accessible, policy-ready datasets that support research, health, and decision-making at the neighbourhood scale.

Participants will explore Imago datasets and learn how to replicate analytical workflows across multiple products. The workshop will begin by demonstrating a complete workflow using an example dataset. Attendees will then apply the same steps to alternative dataset combinations, building confidence in transferring methods.

Working with real data, participants will load spatial boundaries, merge imagery-derived indicators with deprivation measures, produce summary statistics, and create maps and visualisations to identify spatial patterns. The session also introduces simple statistical techniques—including correlation, regression, and hotspot identification—to support evidence-based interpretations while encouraging critical reflection on confounding factors and causal inference.

Designed for all experience levels, the training welcomes those new to satellite data alongside participants familiar with geospatial tools or programming in R or Python. By the end of the workshop, attendees will have a reusable analytical workflow, practical mapping skills, and a clearer understanding of how imagery-derived data can help address real-world social policy questions.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Accessing the Carceral Continuum: Success, Denial and Negotiation (1)
schedule_event.description: Carceral spaces, despite their multiple mobilities and porous patchwork boundaries, remain inherently difficult for researchers to access. Whilst the intricacies of entry differs according to different institutional and national contexts, common to most access attempts is the need to liaise with institutional actors via personal connections or lengthy formal applications. Such negotiations see scholar’s access gatekept, restricted and often denied under the guise that projects are unaligned to official interests or compromise organisational security. Despite having to navigate such access conditions, reflections on such processes are often overlooked within carceral geographic scholarship, with papers instead favouring the sharing of research findings. This session creates space for further discussion around the underexplored experiences of negotiating, gaining and being denied access to carceral space-times. Uncovering how the carceral apparatuses we (attempt) to work alongside operate, govern and control not only those who are incarcerated but also those who study carceral spaces. Engaging with the conference theme of geographies of inequalities, such reflections allow us to address the inequalities which emerge when attempting to do carceral research, and to acknowledge how research restrictions (re)produce inequalities in knowledge regarding the lived experience of incarcerated people.

paper.title: Negotiating prison officers’ role in delivering healthcare: Reflections from a Canadian qualitative study
Tracing urban entanglements across the carceral continuum: geographies of pretrial detention and homelessness in downtown São Paulo
Autoethnography as Access: Bypassing the Gatekeeper Through Lived Experience

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Accessing the Carceral Continuum: Success, Denial and Negotiation (2)
schedule_event.description:

Whilst the intricacies of entry differs according to different institutional and national contexts, common to most access attempts is the need to liaise with institutional actors via personal connections or lengthy formal applications. Uncovering how the carceral apparatuses we (attempt) to work alongside operate, govern and control not only those who are incarcerated but also those who study carceral spaces. Engaging with the conference theme of geographies of inequalities, such reflections allow us to address the inequalities which emerge when attempting to do carceral research, and to acknowledge how research restrictions (re)produce inequalities in knowledge regarding the lived experience of incarcerated people.

This second session will take a workshop format, and will collectively explore the challenges associated with researcher access in carceral contexts, building upon the reflections traced in the paper presentations prior. Specifically, we will ask attendees to consider three themes: success, denial, and negotiation. This will be aimed at all career stages, with the intention of bringing different researchers together to share experiences, knowledges, and solutions to these questions of access. This workshop therefore helping to address the inequalities experienced by different people attempting to do carceral research. As knowledge about access to carceral spaces is often limited, reproducing inequalities for incarcerated people, more collective understanding about access processes, and what a lack of access can tell us, can enable us to work towards more just spaces. We hope after the session to produce a resource that collates our collective reflections, to be used as a valuable tool for carceral scholars going forward. We will make this resource available through the Carceral Geographies Working Group network.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Participation in the City: How Urban Participatory Innovations Are Reshaping Power and Democracy
schedule_event.description:
Cities are crucial arenas where democracy is contested, reimagined, and reshaped, innovating new practices and institutions of participation. Yet we still lack a clear understanding of how urban life anddemocratic practice.. This panel examines what we term “urban participatory innovations” (UPIs): hybrid, collective efforts to reconfigure decision-making, transform social reproduction, and reshape power relations in urban contexts.

Drawing on insights from our forthcoming book Participation in the City, the panel brings together interdisciplinary research from seven cities across four regions—Buenos Aires, Cape Town, Lyon, New York, São Paulo, Toronto, and Warsaw—developed by a team of over 25 scholars. Through a comparative lens, we investigate how UPIs create new democratic ecosystems, challenge existing hierarchies, and produce new solidarities amid crises of urban inequality and democratic legitimacy.

Rather than reducing participation to either institutional reform or grassroots mobilization, we explore its complex interplay with urban governance, informality, digital engagement, and struggles over territory. In line with RGS 2026’s theme “Geographies of inequalities: towards just places.,” we ask: How do UPIs cultivate (in) justice in the city? How does participation impact urban inequalities? What ruptures in power and governance do UPIs enable? And how might they prefigure more just urban futures?

This panel offers new conceptual frameworks and empirical insights that not only advance scholarly debates but also inform practitioners, policymakers, and activists seeking to harness urban spaces as arenas of democratic renewal.
