schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Emerging frontiers of urban popular economies
schedule_event.description:
There has been renewed attention to the plurality of economic forms through which people organise their livelihoods in economic geography and related fields. Concepts such as variegated capitalism (Peck and Theodore, 2007) or diverse economies (Gibson-Graham, 2008; Cameron and Gibson-Graham, 2022) have emerged to describe and analyse the actually existing economy, characterised by a diverse, heterogeneous, and often hybrid set of practices that sustain everyday life. One of such notions is the concept of popular economies, which has a longer genealogy rooted in the Latin American critique of the informal economy. Rather than a marginal sector destined to disappear, the popular economy was conceptualised as an alternative way of organising the economy (Coraggio, 1992; Razeto et al., 1983). Central to this perspective are the articulation between economic production and social reproduction, and the coexistence of cooperation and solidarity with competition. More recently, the concept has been reworked to account for how popular economies are reshaped by, and actively reappropriate, neoliberal rationalities such as financialisation, entrepreneurialism, and market expansion (Gago, 2017). Increasingly, the concept has been taken up more widely and applied to urban geographies (Simone, 2019), travelling beyond Latin America. This call is motivated by the recognition of emerging dynamics in urban popular economies, such as their growing transnationalisation, alongside other transformations and shifting frontiers that are reshaping urban life.

Late Asian industrialisation and rise of Global China have fuelled urban popular economies with cheap goods, expanding their territorial reach into globalised economic circuits, as well as introducing new agents across different transnational spaces. Additionally, intensified migration has transformed the lived realities of these urban economies. Many migrants find livelihoods on the street and in popular markets, whereas the coexistence of diverse groups generates new opportunities for conflict and cross-cultural cooperation and solidarity. Also intertwined with popular economies are other major contemporary trends, such as the rise of platform capitalism and e-commerce, the centrality of illicit economies in many urban contexts, and the rise of political extremism and hostility against immigrants and ethnic minorities. How are these overlapping and often contradictory dynamics reshaping popular economies, their territories of operation, their moral logics, and their political possibilities?

This session invites contributions that engage critically with Urban Popular Economy Frontiers, understood as sites of expansion, tension, and struggles. We welcome papers that explore, challenge, or rethink the concept of urban popular economies, its geographical extension, new empirical applications (in and beyond Latin America) and emerging methodological strategies in light of contemporary transformations.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Un/translatable Geographies: querying the politics and ontologies of knowledge production in Latin America
schedule_event.description:
Gambiarra, jeitinho, mestizaje, machismo, territorio, buen vivir – sumak kawsay. In recent years, scholars publishing in English language have increasingly turned to specific notions emerging from fieldwork to contribute to geographical and broader academic debates. From marginal words in brackets or buried in fieldnotes, untranslatable notions have become centrepieces of empirical works and conceptual propositions. They are used to name and explain everyday practices and the complex social, material, and historical configurations in which they emerge, reflecting efforts to provincialise and decolonise knowledge (Yates and Nuñez 2021; Sultana 2025).

However, the circulation of these concepts is not uncontested. While situated concepts can pluralise notions of being and knowing the world, they can also repeat problematic patterns of exclusion and appropriation in the production of geographical knowledge. When moved across contexts, these concepts can shift, misalign, or even thicken. As they travel and become entangled in English-speaking geographies, these concepts’ textures can flatten; their singularity, irreducibly situated (Jazeel, 2019), might become domesticated. This risks reproducing epistemic hierarchies across locales, between theory and “case,” and between concepts that travel easily and those that remain marginal.

This session invites scholars to think with the untranslatable in geographical research. Staying with the contradictions, paradoxes and dilemmas of knowledge production in English-speaking, Northern academia, it asks whether conceptual travel can generate productive misalignments, unsettling the status quo of knowledge production. Thinking with situated concepts, which are, for the lack of a better word, chi’ixi (Rivera Cusicanqui 2012), the session queries whether the mobilisation of concepts beyond their original contexts can open analytical, political, and relational horizons: energizing connection without sameness, and theory that remains accountable to place.

Following the short interactive paper format (short presentations of five minutes each, followed by a chaired interactive discussion involving all the presenters and the audience to explore common themes from the papers),  presenters will tell a story about their engagement with one particular ‘untranslatable’ concept and how this speaks to three topics: analytical and political power; epistemic and geo-political hierarchies; and the ontological. Our goal is to have a discussion grounded in ethnographic material that engages critically with the un/translatable, asking not only what is lost, but also what becomes possible when concepts move.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Pessimist Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
From pessimist-philosopher Arthur Schopenhauer’s declaration (2000: 309) that “there is no measure or end of suffering” to the afro-pessimist insistence on the need for “the end of this world” (Wilderson, 2018), pessimism has manifold lives. Whether as a psychological disposition or collective affect, an indifference to the world or a call to arms, pessimism names the absence or loss of an “orientation toward a future, toward something always yet to come, conceived as bestowing a value on life by way of the future anterior” (Berlant and Edelman, 2014: 3).

Despite their habits of depleting and exhausting, pessimisms appear, currently, to be flourishing, attaching themselves to subjects, narratives, and places, for better or for worse. The central questions this session is concerned with is: How do (and which) times and spaces appear to invite pessimism, and which exclude it? What kinds of (non)relations, destitutions, or affects come to matter when something, or someone, enters into a pessimist relationship to others, the self, or the world as a whole? How do pessimisms come to structure or fracture subjects, places, communities, politics, and temporalities?

An expert in “raising problems without solutions,” (Thacker, 2015: 5) the pessimist often becomes the problem, and their pessimism becomes simply a matter of failure: a failure to see the light at the end of the tunnel, a failure of political and philosophical thinking, a failure to locate and embrace the joys hidden in the abundant present, a failure to pick oneself up, a failure to build in a crisis-ridden present. Without feeling the need to ascribe pessimism such a moral value or a prescriptive ethics (e.g. of transformation, of rehabilitation), we invite examinations that stay with pessimism, how it comes to take hold, and how it (re)shapes our sense of self, the social, politics, and the world. Paying attention to pessimism brings us not only into its own labour, but also into that of optimism as that which commonly propels, and prescribes, our social relations, political narratives, collective dreams, personal projects, and societal organisations.

paper.title: Philosophical moods and the ‘wheel of critique’. Whither a negative Stimmung?
Misaligning pessimism: discursive constructions of global and positive tipping points

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Who knows? Inequality and Intersectionality in Knowledge Production (1)
schedule_event.description:
This panel interrogates the intersectionality of gendered, classed, racialized and institutional dynamics shape the production, circulation, and definition of knowledge and technology. Feminist scholarship has shown how knowledge has been unequally produced to both entrench socio-economic inequalities and challenge institutional hegemony (Murphy 2012; Massey, 1995). The inequality of knowledge is further reproduced based on the dynamics of power and imaginations, through the accumulation of history and contemporary practices co-constitutive with spatial processes of differentiation and the exclusion of subject and object (Gregory, 1998; Liebman et al. 2023, Datta, 2023). While the (re)production of knowledge reproduces a hierarchy that further intensifies economic, health and social inequalities, people negotiate through everyday practices to challenge the existing regime of knowledge (Qureshi et al, 2017).

This session advances critical approaches to historical and contemporary topics grounded in the intersections of gender, race and class in knowledge (re)production. Such topics include, conflicts, appropriations and negotiations between informality and expertise, autonomy and epistemic in/justices, transnational and (de)colonial knowledge production/management, intimate technologies and domestic labour and bottom-up strategies.

Accordingly, this session will discuss diverse cases of knowledge, technology, power and in/justice to grapple with these complexities and identify modes of intervention into such inequalities as geographers. Enriching feminist geographic scholarship on the gendered politics of knowledge production and information circulation, this session brings together case studies across different time periods and geographical areas to focus on the inequality in the geography of knowledge, and the conflicts and negotiation in formal and informal practices. Who is qualified as the subject of knowledge and who defines it? What conflicts and collaborations occur in the process of negotiation, appropriation and translation of knowledge? How does time and space influence the dynamics of knowledge?

This panel is the first part of two panels. The first panel features four papers. The second session will feature the last two papers. A roundtable with all authors from both panels will close the session.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Who knows? Inequality and Intersectionality in Knowledge Production (2)
schedule_event.description:
This panel interrogates the intersectionality of gendered, classed, racialized and institutional dynamics shape the production, circulation, and definition of knowledge and technology. Feminist scholarship has shown how knowledge has been unequally produced to both entrench socio-economic inequalities and challenge institutional hegemony (Murphy 2012; Massey, 1995). The inequality of knowledge is further reproduced based on the dynamics of power and imaginations, through the accumulation of history and contemporary practices co-constitutive with spatial processes of differentiation and the exclusion of subject and object (Gregory, 1998; Liebman et al. 2023, Datta, 2023). While the (re)production of knowledge reproduces a hierarchy that further intensifies economic, health and social inequalities, people negotiate through everyday practices to challenge the existing regime of knowledge (Qureshi et al, 2017).

This session advances critical approaches to historical and contemporary topics grounded in the intersections of gender, race and class in knowledge (re)production. Such topics include, conflicts, appropriations and negotiations between informality and expertise, autonomy and epistemic in/justices, transnational and (de)colonial knowledge production/management, intimate technologies and domestic labour and bottom-up strategies.

Accordingly, this session will discuss diverse cases of knowledge, technology, power and in/justice to grapple with these complexities and identify modes of intervention into such inequalities as geographers. Enriching feminist geographic scholarship on the gendered politics of knowledge production and information circulation, this session brings together case studies across different time periods and geographical areas to focus on the inequality in the geography of knowledge, and the conflicts and negotiation in formal and informal practices. Who is qualified as the subject of knowledge and who defines it? What conflicts and collaborations occur in the process of negotiation, appropriation and translation of knowledge? How does time and space influence the dynamics of knowledge?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Contestations of green transitions in UK agriculture (1)
schedule_event.description:
The green agricultural transition in the UK is at a fractious, contested, and pivotal moment. Post-Brexit changes to agri-environmental schemes have yet to realise significant success in minimising the environmental impact of agriculture and have led to farmers' protests across the UK. While much of the UK's net-zero plans rely on support  from rural (and farming) communities, reactionary groups have exploited farmers' feelings of uncertainty, frustration, and a lack of trust in the government to promote anti-net-zero sentiments . The notion of a just transition is key to any future green transition in UK agriculture, yet how this transition can be realised, and what a "just future" even looks like, remains unclear.

This session explores the possibilities threats, opportunities, limitations of, and resistances to green agricultural transitions within the UK. Themes include but are not limited to:

  • Farmers' perspectives on climate change and the green transition
  • The role of experiential knowledge in a achieving a just transition in agriculture
  • Farmers' protests and the attempted co-option of these protests by reactionary groups
  • Policy or financial barriers to agricultural transitions
  • Visions for a "just future" of farming

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Interventions in and with environmental publics: Creative approaches to renegotiating (un)just places (1)
schedule_event.description:
In policy and practice across diverse sectors, environments are abstracted from material realities and lived experiences, enabling environmental and socioeconomic injustices. In academia, place-based and participatory creative approaches have emerged as counter-hegemonic attempts to ground ways of understanding people, places, and the more-than-human (McLean, 2022). In recent years, calls within both Geography (Basile, 2025) and the Environmental Humanities (Van Dooren et al., 2024) for scholarship to engage with publics have brought such approaches into dialogue with policy and practice (e.g. Davis and de Andre, 2025). For scholars, introducing participatory creative methods into applied contexts promises ‘more inclusive and empowering socioenvironmental possibilities’ (Van Dooren et al., 2024, p.7), while also presenting methodological and epistemological challenges.

This session invites interdisciplinary contributions and asks:

  • How can participatory creative methods translate local knowledge into actionable policy and practice interventions that contribute to the creation of more just places?
  • How do the affective, relational, and reflexive ways of knowing environments enabled by participatory creative methods challenge dominant frameworks in policy and practice?
  • How can these methods centre collaboration and facilitate reciprocity with marginalised communities?
  • What are the ethics and politics of representing environments and their inhabitants through these methods?

In a time of increasing socioenvironmental inequalities, this session responds to the need for more situated, pluralistic, and textured understandings of place beyond the academy.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Agricultural land and the Urban Periphery (1)
schedule_event.description:
Urban scholars argue that the periphery must take center stage in urban enquiry, especially in the Global South where most urbanisation occurs through the conversion of agricultural lands (Gururani, 2020; Kennedy & Sood, 2016a). Far from being a residual or marginal space, it has become a key terrain for mega-projects, world-class smart cities, and speculative real estate (Goldman, 2011; Kennedy et al., 2014; Raman, 2016). Periphery is shaped through the continuous interactions between rural and urban actors, institutions, and processes (Balakrishnan & Gururani, 2021). The promised idea of development through conversion of agricultural lands is often portrayed as future-oriented, and marketised neatly in form of planned development, smart cities, high-rise gated residential neighbourhoods, and shopping centres. However, this future oriented imagination is often complex and layered with historical past, and present. This is particularly relevant in context of periphery, where land, memory, and kinship remain deeply interwoven in form of colonial land regimes, ancestral claims to land, common lands, or historical associations to land (Simpson, 2014). This also sometimes becomes a subtle form of resistance to the future-oriented disciplining of planned development projected through policies and master plans.

The inherited inequalities due to differential power relations because of social, political, spatial, and economic relations gets refigured during these transitions of agricultural lands at the urban periphery. This largely aligns with the theme of the conference focusing on complexities of spatial inequalities at the periphery. Through this call for proposal, we seek to invite papers across various geographies undergoing transformation of agricultural lands on the periphery. We are looking for papers that engage conceptually, methodologically, or empirically grounded with the themes including (not limited to): temporality, contestation of land use changes, role of state, landownership and tenure, and social and cultural relations associated with agricultural lands. This could include exploring questions around:

  • What are the different forms of practices rooted in past or present that actors engage on agricultural lands?
  • How do these practices interact with the future oriented planned development envisioned through the conversion of agricultural lands?
  • Do these practices contest, resist, or further widen the inherited power asymmetries associated with agricultural lands?
  • What are the methods that can evoke the past relation or absences and compel the actors to push forward or construct the image of lands in transition against the future oriented maps or images produced through policies or plans?
  • How do we capture the emotions, the visuals, and the spatial-temporal changes taken place at the periphery that weaves together the past, present, and future?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Advancing Geographies of Wellbeing: Critical, Contextual and Qualitative Perspectives
schedule_event.description:
In recent years, geographical work has sought to conceptualise wellbeing as relational, emergent and processual: as an embodied, affective and atmospheric experience, always located in time and space. Drawing on non-representational and posthumanist approaches, this scholarship has significantly expanded how wellbeing is understood. This work responds to earlier critiques of dominant wellbeing research, including its individualism and reliance on narrow quantitative parameters, as well as its assumption that wellbeing is knowable, attainable and able to be increased. There have also been critical evaluations of positive psychology, given its focus on acquiring skills for happiness, and the neoliberal ideologies that shift blame and responsibility from systemic processes to individuals. 

Responding to these observations, the papers in this panel advance geographies of wellbeing in several ways, drawing on critical, contextual and qualitative perspectives. At a high level, we ask whether wellbeing remains a useful concept, given the lack of definitional consensus, while considering whether its semantic malleability might increase its utility.  We also recognise that for many marginalised groups – including asylum seekers, migrants, prisoners and other minorities – struggles over rights, recognition, safety and material survival often take precedence over abstract or normative accounts of ‘living well’ or ‘happiness’. In such contexts, the language of wellbeing risks obscuring structural violence, depoliticising inequality, or imposing exclusionary and culturally specific ideals. In the contexts of extreme inequality and marginalisation, discussions of ‘wellbeing’ may seem disconnected from lived experience and of little immediate relevance.

Through the papers and related discussion, we will engage critically with the ontology and epistemology of wellbeing, considering what is at stake in retaining, reformulating, and even moving beyond the term altogether.  We will explore novel methodologies for researching and evaluating wellbeing, and for studying embodied, relational and more-than-human experiences. We will also examine the potential of creative approaches for understanding what it means to be well and how we, as researchers, can apply these methods with sensitivity.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Beyond post-colonial approaches in climate change adaptation: a critical look at decolonisation-induced inequalities
schedule_event.description:
Decolonisation has increasingly become a buzzword of post-colonial scholars and institutions but, how approaches promoted by this concept enhance inclusiveness is more challenging and less understood. This is based on critically addressing how current climate change adaptation governance continues to largely exploit and marginalise vulnerable nations and communities in the Global South and North.

Decolonising climate governance is critical and urgent to create alternative narratives, institutions, and practices that challenge the dominant ideology and power structures. However, it is fundamentally naive to assume that all decolonising or Indigenous-led climate scholarship and practice represents or benefits all members of traditional marginalised indigenous or local groups. Decolonisation comes with the risk of societal fragmentation and widening inequalities. This happens when initiatives are captured by special interests and elites, as a result of unequal power relations. This could lead to unintended consequences such as the exclusion or disempowerment of marginalised or less powerful groups.

This session contributes to a small but growing body of work that attends to multiple subjectivities within marginalised indigenous and local communities. The session seeks to engage in scholarship that examines how power differentials at the intersection of different positionalities and intersectionalities produce differing experiences of climate change adaptation from a decolonised perspective. Contributions from both Global South and Global North contexts, and from diverse methodological approaches, are especially welcome. To ensure that decolonisation in climate change adaptation remains relevant to Indigenous and local people, this session explores intersectionalities in decolonised climate change adaptation by welcoming contributions that explore:

  • Empirical cases of decolonisation in climate change adaptation within the domains of water, energy, food and health.
  • Types of collective interests sustained and perpetuated following decolonisation processes.
  • Inequalities and exclusions produced and reproduced in the process of adaptation.
  • How individuals and communities are resisting inequalities created by decolonisation processes.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Cultivating Just Community Food Systems through Food Sharing (1)
schedule_event.description:
Socio-economic, environmental and spatial inequalities are central to how Food Sharing Initiatives (FSIs), from community gardens and landed kitchens, to seed saving initiatives and community composting, develop, organize, and operate. These inequalities shape the landscapes that FSIs are embedded in, as well as their strategies for inclusion, advocacy, and transformation.

Spatial inequalities in land access and governance, for example, directly influence the ways in which collective growing initiatives access and govern land, and which populations they are accountable to. For example, in cities across the US racialized disinvestments in black neighborhoods through red lining are linked to an abundance of vacant land and the demand for community gardens. At the same times, spatial and socio-economic inequalities that manifest in gentrification and land speculation can make community gardens unwitting accomplices in green gentrification. Under conditions of climatic change, multilayered riskscapes increasingly affect adaptive capacities around community food initiatives internationally, with community gardens rebranded as urban nature-based solutions in some locations.

The COVID 19 pandemic revealed the fragility of the capitalist food system, and the resilience of the community food system – where countless forms of mutual aid (from community fridges to community kitchens) mobilized to rescue and redistribute food and/or access food from local growers, to feed to those in need, alongside more established food banks and food aid mechanisms. Socio-economic and power inequalities are central to how food sharing is organized, beginning from the economic inequalities that create the need for food assistance, to the power inequalities between food donors and receivers, to social inequalities and stigmas related to charity and surplus food.

In this session we invite food sharing scholars and community-based food practitioners to reflect on the role of food sharing initiatives in creating and sustaining just places. We challenge contributors to address inequalities and injustices that shape how these initiatives develop and operate, and what injustices might still need to be overcome for FSIs to be successful in meeting their mission, and creating more just community food systems and places. We are interested in approaches and tools developed to be a counterweight to inequalities and the extent to which food sharing initiatives collaborative with other actors in their efforts to do this. We are keen to hear about diverse community food imaginaries and research exploring tensions between these and those expressed by public or private sector actors.

We welcome theoretically and empirically driven papers (or more creative interventions that can be shared in 15 minutes) that address topics such as:

Art and citizen engagement

Serious Games

Food sharing

Food governance

Food Infrastructure

Food Commons

Food sustainability

Care

Conviviality

Community cooking & eating

Community growing

Surplus Food Redistribution

Agroecological urbanism

Landed Community Kitchens

Communities of food practice

Critical food mapping

Community food imaginaries

Collective food action

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Historicising Urban Labour Regimes: In/Visibility, Infrastructural Labour, and Resistance (1)
schedule_event.description:
The relationship between urbanisation and (semi)proletarianisation reflects distinct historical conjunctures shaped by colonialism, racial capitalism, and the uneven insertion of economies into global circuits of capital. These processes have produced urban labour regimes in which informality, precarity, household-based production, and circular migration are not transitional anomalies but enduring and politically consequential forms of urban life.

Despite precarious workers' contributions to urban reproduction through 'essential' infrastructural labour (Fredericks, 2014, 2021; Gidwani, 2015; Gidwani and Chari, 2004; Stokes and De-Coss Corzo, 2023), worker histories and historical struggles remain underexplored in geographical debate. In the archival register, we find scant information of worker lives in records of the colonial and postcolonial state, and in the ethnographic register, the social reproductive realm and unwaged or ‘hidden’ labour are often treated as less important or accessible relative to the politics of waged work and the worksite. Increasingly precarious conditions—exacerbated by climate stress, privatization, debt, xenophobia, authoritarianism and surveillance—make tracing these histories and contemporary realities ever more pressing.

This panel invites historically grounded and empirically diverse contributions across geographies and sectors that foreground comparative approaches to urban labour regimes. It asks: how have different historical pathways of urbanisation shaped labour relations and struggles in southern cities across class, gender, caste and race? We invite reflections on whether Southern urban labour histories might illuminate contemporary Northern urban conditions marked by platform economies, migrant labour preponderance, and social reproduction infrastructure transformations. However, we also question the risks of universalising labour categories and temporal narratives. How might historicising urban labour differently enable more situated, worker-centred theories of urban development?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Diaspora Diplomacy: Crafting a More-Than-State Geopolitics?
schedule_event.description:
The questions this session asks are the following: how do diasporas develop their own diplomacies and participate in a broader geopolitical worldmaking beyond the state? And what are the geographies of their diplomatic practices?

While the new US administration and its ‘Donroe Doctrine’ are shaping the world towards what has been defined as “hemispheric geopolitics” (Dodds, 2026), the rise of global diasporas (Cohen, 2023) in international politics seems to keep blurring the boundaries of territorial sovereignty. Their growing involvement in international politics has led scholars to speak of both human geopolitics (Gamlen, 2019) and diasporic geopolitics (Adamson and Han, 2024), arguing that they will be a key element in the future international order. Diasporas constitute a terrain for competition between states, but also for discovering opportunities to rethink diplomatic practices and reshape geopolitics within and beyond the state.

Due to their liminal subjectivity emerging from participation in multiple worlds (McConnell, 2017), today, diasporas act as proactive participants in bilateral, multilateral, and polylateral diplomacy alongside other non-state actors, while also being increasingly subject to extraterritorial governance and surveillance by their home states (Gamlen et al., 2019).

This consolidation in diaspora activism and governance has given rise to a new term, debated in both policy and academic circles: diaspora diplomacy. Geographers have built on earlier critiques of state-centric approaches that framed diaspora populations as passive agents of homeland development and foreign policy, instead focusing on forms of diplomacy enacted by diasporas (Boyle & Ho, 2017; Ho & McConnell, 2019). The field of diaspora diplomacy explores unconventional geographies and practices of worldmaking, diplomatic relations, diplomatic training, and internationalist solidarities and institutions, interweaving state and non-state actors.

Examining the role of diasporas in diplomacy can shed light on how complex international relations operate in practice. In particular, a focus on diaspora diplomacy can highlight innovative modes and instruments for building a more cooperative transnational space and for giving voice to those who are often marginalized in the international arena. Diaspora diplomacy compels us to challenge rigid assumptions and categories – such as the internal/external and territorialization/deterritorialization dichotomies – while also prompting a rethinking of both statehood and non-statehood.

This session welcomes theoretical and empirical contributions that help explore diaspora diplomacy, particularly through critical approaches.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Doing diplomacy beyond national jurisdiction (1)
schedule_event.description:
Environmental diplomacy is inherently geographical (see Kuus, 2016). It takes place; it is geographically placed (in specific nations, cities, and then in particular buildings (Touloumi, 2024), etc., as well as online (i.e. Vadrot et al. 2021)), and it involves hierarchies of socio-political place, rendered through power dynamics (i.e. ‘weaker’ states vs. ‘powerful’ ones, global north and south, ‘developing’, ‘developed’, ‘small island’ and so on). Diplomacy, then, involves inequalities of access, across different scales, from restricted institutional decision-making spaces (such as the UN) to places beyond national jurisdiction that are being discussed (i.e. the High Seas).

This session will pick apart the practices of researching in spaces of environmental diplomacy and bring to the fore difficulties encountered in undertaking research by actively engaging in spaces of environmental diplomacy (Hughes and Vadrot, 2023). In doing so, it also addresses how researchers might have to ‘do’ research diplomatically. It also considers the access points for those involved directly in diplomacy and decision-making.

In proposing this session, we take inspiration from the recent ratification of the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction (BBNJ Treaty), the ongoing negotiation of a Mining Code for the seabed beyond national jurisdiction by the International Seabed Authority (ISA), and a year of yet more global level environmental conferencing (i.e. COP and United Nations Ocean Conference (UNOC)). We invite submissions that deal with all iterations of environmental diplomacy, however, with a focus on the governance of geographies beyond national jurisdiction.

Organisations such as the United Nations (UN) and annual meetings, including the Conference of the Parties (COP) and the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic and the Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), rely on individual nations to act as host countries. Meeting attendance is subject to certain delegate status, from visa costs and approvals, security guardrails, to adhering to the host country’s governance regimes. Membership and participation in meetings are often exclusive, resource-intensive, multilevelled, and deeply uneven across delegations and events, with convoluted processes for gaining access and limitations on speaking rights. We thus ask who gets to make (legally-binding) decisions, and on behalf of whom, as many of these negotiations tackle issues that concern us all.

paper.title: Introduction to Doing diplomacy beyond national jurisdiction

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of neighbourhood poverty and deprivation: Examining compounding and intersecting insecurities
schedule_event.description:
Geographers have played an important role in examining the spatial differentiation of poverty and deprivation in a variety of urban areas. Popularised concepts such as ‘leftbehind’ neighbourhoods (Houlden et al, 2024), ‘neighbourhood effects’ (Bauder, 2002), ‘contextual poverty’ (Andersson et al, 2023) and ‘urban marginality’ (Thieme, Lancione & Rosa, 2017) illustrate how poverty and deprivation are often spatially concentrated at the neighbourhood scale, often intensifying in hyper-local pockets, such as streets or estates. These neighbourhoods are shaped by processes of marginalisation and their intersections with different spatial scales and contexts, such as wider institutional and policy environments (Petrović et al, 2021). They are often characterised by low-income households who experience limited socio-economic outcomes and life-chances.

In this session, we will explore diverse geographies of poverty and
deprivation at the neighbourhood and hyper-local scale. We are particularly keen to
examine the compounding and intersecting insecurities in such geographies.

In such neighbourhoods, people often experience compounding (accumulating) and
intersecting (overlapping) insecurities that go beyond living on a low-income (Bucelli et al,
2025; Ragin & Fiss, 2016). One insecurity can lead to another, with the impacts intensifying and multiplying. Systemic and socio-economic factors that compound and intersect include employment insecurity, precarious housing, inadequate infrastructure, welfare restrictions, and limited access to public services. Socio-cultural factors can include stigma, racism, social cohesion/fragmentation, and limited social infrastructures.

In this session, we aim to explore why and how compounding and intersecting insecurities emerge and persist and the impacts they have on people’s everyday lives and opportunities. We are interested in examining how neighbourhoods of poverty and deprivation are made and how people’s experiences are shaped by multiple spatial scales and contexts. Additionally, we are keen to examine how people and local organisations respond to, negotiate and aim to mitigate against compounding and intersecting insecurities.

Responses may be more top-down, such as interventions from local and national
governments to more locally led ones, such as interventions from charities, voluntary and
community organisations and religious groups. We also want to examine how people, on anindividual level, negotiate insecurities in their everyday lives. At the heart of this session, we want to explore the granular details of disadvantaged places and how insecurities and inequalities intersect, compound and intensify.

Neighbourhood experiences and responses to compounding insecurities in Sunderland, UK
Multidimensional hardship; contextualising faith-based responses to inequality and insecurity in one northern-English neighbourhood
Life Course Stratification in Space and Time: Opportunity Structures and the Reproduction of Inequality
Measuring Multiple Deprivation in the Canton of Zurich

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Governing the AI-urban-environment nexus
schedule_event.description:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly positioned within technocratic narratives as a pathway toward sustainable and resilient urban futures, from air-quality monitoring and heat-risk prediction to flood-warning systems and strategic visions for climate change mitigation. Yet, this framing simultaneously reshapes how environmental governance is both enacted and imagined in cities, privileging data-driven, anticipatory, and predictive decision-making for managing urban ecological uncertainty and environmental risks (Cugurullo and Xu, 2025; Marvin, 2025), while sidelining and obscuring more diverse, adaptive, context-specific approaches (Das et al., forthcoming).

AI does not merely subject the urban environment to new forms of algorithmic control; it also materially transforms it (Bratton, 2021). The expansion of AI infrastructures demands significant energy resources, generates heat, and produces large volumes of e-waste (Cugurullo et al., 2025). These environmental impacts are unevenly distributed, reflecting and reinforcing existing social, geopolitical, and infrastructural inequalities (Fard, 2020). The integration of AI into urban environmental governance thus presents a paradox: while promoted as a technological solution to environmental issues, AI simultaneously introduces new layers of ecological degradation and environmental vulnerability (Das and Woods, forthcoming). From the cooling of data centres to the energy consumed by AI models, the entanglements of AI and environmental governance unfold across multiple dimensions of urban life. In this context, it remains unclear whether AI technologies can be actually capable of improving urban sustainability. Nonetheless, lured by the promises of the ideology of AI, city managers from all around the world are investing considerable amount of resources in the construction of large AI infrastructures supposed to make urban living more sustainable (Cugurullo, 2025).

Moreover, AI-enabled environmental governance can reproduce or intensify socio-spatial inequalities. Overreliance on AI and algorithmic decision-making can overlook context-sensitive knowledge, reinforce data biases, and further marginalize vulnerable communities (Smith, 2020; Arora et al., 2023) particularly in Global South cities where lack of access to resources, uneven impacts of climate change, and infrastructural gaps are already pronounced. Consequently, the design, deployment, and governance of AI technologies must consider environmental and social impacts, especially at the local scale where environmental governance can shape how AI is being integrated into urban contexts.

This session will explore both conceptual and empirical contributions that examine how AI deployment is increasingly shaping, and being shaped by, urban environmental governance. It will critically analyze the applications, imaginaries, infrastructures, environmental impacts, governance frameworks, and emergent frictions at the intersection of AI and urban environmental governance.

paper.title: Frictions and Fictions of Urban Environmental AI: On digital twins, urban governance, and the impossibility of a finished map (but the useful appeal therein)

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Justice in Tourism Geographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
Despite a long-held concern with issues of justice within geography, in recent years we have witnessed a growing recognition that justice is a core concern among tourism geographers (cf. Paddison & Hall, 2022; Tomassini & Lamond, 2023; Wan et. al., 2024). And yet, despite its high usage, there is often limited direct discussion from tourism geographers regarding the substance of justice. Geographers, including those working in tourism studies, have often been hesitant to articulate what they mean by ‘justice,’ concerned that doing so imposes a normative ideal of fairness. Instead, they tend to favour practice-based, pluriversal, ‘bottom-up’ understandings of justice (Barnett, 2016); a move that constructs a universal/pluriversal binary.

In the past decade, however, geographers have cautioned that there is a requirement to openly discuss the substance of justice, to consider the specificities of claims in relation to another, avoid extreme relativism whereby all claims to justice are equally valid without grounds for critique, and steer clear of any reductions in the concept’s political and analytical utility (Barnett, 2016; Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). We draw on this critical commentary to call for open dialogue within the sub-discipline of tourism geographies, to the conditions through which injustices are recognised and addressed, to make sense of ‘how and why observable patterns of inequity, discrimination, or unevenness are actually unjust’ (Barnett, 2016, pp.112).

paper.title: The Reproduction of Pro-Growth Tourism through the Legitimisation of Spatial and Temporal Dispersal
Justice as a Constitutive Principle of Tourism Governance: A Metagovernance Perspective on Structural Inequality
Reclaiming Sacred Ground: Unjust Tourism Growth and the Cheapening of Lives and Nature in Labuan Bajo
Inclusivity in future-oriented tourism development

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Cities and the Disinformation Age?
schedule_event.description:
Since the late 1990s the shift from ‘industrialism’ to ‘informationalism’ ushered in proclamations that a new world of ‘knowledge capitalism’ had dawned, in which knowledge and information was the resource for economic competitiveness and societal development (May and Perry 2018). Cities were seen at the vanguard of this shift, whether as active proponents or sites where alternative imaginaries for a more democratic ‘knowledge society’ could emerge. These opposing struggles – for knowledge as a tool to extract value or as a lever for spatial justice – nonetheless aligned in framing ‘knowledge’ and ‘information’ as (relatively) unproblematic categories. Orthodox scholarship was oriented to how cities could enable spillovers and agglomeration economies to leverage profitable advantage, whilst critique focused on spatial inequalities (Madanipour 2011) resulting from the intensification of techno-capitalist extractions from the ‘urban field’ (Moisio and Rossi 2025).

Are we now in a disinformation age or ‘infodemic’ (Stephens et al. 2023)? Has the promise of the information age (Bell 1979; Castells 2010) turned sour in the midst of the contemporary geopolitical landscape – where what counts as ‘knowledge’ and ‘information’, as much as what we do with it, is contested and politicised? Disinformation is not new, but the speed, scale and intensity of changes raises fresh questions, given the intersections between rising authoritarianism, democratic backsliding and technologies and tools to ‘control the capital’ (Goodfellow and Jackman 2023), digital and technological innovations (Balayan and Tomin 2024), their re-appropriation as mechanisms for urban control (Schlumberger et al. 2024) and the geopolitical restructuring of global relations. There is emerging but underdeveloped scholarship – often outside urban geography - on the role of different local populations and institutions in resisting ‘truth decay’ (Jackson 2025) in the context of diminishing trust in democracy.

This session brings together scholars within and outside geography and urban studies who are deploying and challenging extant ideas and discourses of 'disinformation' in different cities: from AI hype and disinformation in making future cities in Hyderabad (India), infopolitics in Abidjan (Cote d’Ivoire), the political economy of disinformation in metropolitan regions in India, everyday information access between and within communities in Tripoli (Lebanon), Patna (India) and Arua (Uganda), and narratives of disinformation in Dhaka (Bangladesh). Together, we will explore traditional and popular manifestations of dis (and mis)information, recent phenomena and underpinning crises of information access, and the relations between cities and urban infrastructures in the disinformation age.

paper.title: AI Hype and the Speculative City: Notes from Hyderabad
Organised Disinformation: Infopolitics of Uncertainty in Abidjan, Cote d’Ivoire
Crises, Communications and Inequalities: Analyses of Social Media Siloes and Resource Access from Lebanon, India and Uganda

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Climate and extraction: Political ecology of inequality and responsibility
schedule_event.description:
Convenors: 

  • Prof. Anja Nygren, University of Helsinki, (corresponding convenor), anja.nygren@helsinki.fi
  • Associate Prof. Dinah Rajak, University of Sussex, d.r.rajak@sussex.ac.uk

Discussant: 

  • Senior Research Fellow, Dr. Gediminas Lesutis, University of Berlin


A crucial question in confronting climate change and social inequality is how to repair ruined environments and drastically altered livelihoods and lifeworlds in areas affected by large-scale resource extraction. This panel focuses on efforts at repair and claims for responsibility at extractive frontlines, suffering from multifaceted environmental and social effects of resource exploitation. Some of these effects occur gradually, cumulating over time; others in the aftermath of sudden disasters such as oil spills, forest fires, or devastating floods.

We argue that novel approaches are needed to bridge the analyses of environmental restoration initiatives with perspectives that confront the sociopolitical challenges of repair, including unequal distribution of benefits and burdens, uneven power relations, dispossession, traumas of loss, and relations of mistrust. Our panel seeks to contribute to the political ecology of repair and responsibility in three ways. First, we acknowledge the risks involved in neoliberal efforts at double-valuing nature – by extracting it and by repairing it – through accumulation by restoration. The political ecology of repair and responsibility indicates integrated attention to restoration of damaged environments and recovery from dispossession.

Second, recovery from environmental-social harms is interlinked to questions of responsibility, including who should be held accountable for them and take responsibility of repair. We emphasize the struggles for responsibility as multitemporal processes that are historically rooted and temporally grounded, meanwhile entailing forward-thinking for creating new forms of political engagement and institutional recognition. This requires paying attention to how legacies of extraction condition possibilities for change; analysing both the forms of selective remembering and creative reinvention in the wake of extraction and quest for transformative responsibility.

Third, more attention needs to be paid to the efforts of extraction-affected people to reimagine their lifeworlds, reclaim their resource rights and restitch their livelihoods in the pursuit of justice and hope. These efforts need to be examined within the wider political-economic conditions, where dominant discourses and politics often erase alternative ways of thinking and living. Only by linking the analyses of the effects of extractivism with the multi-scalar efforts at restitution it is possible to understand the prospects and constraints involved.

Our session convenes presentations that examine the following topics:

  •  the ways in which people living at the extractive frontlines reimagine and recraft their altered environments, livelihoods, and lifeworlds
  • new avenues for understanding responsibility
  • novel modes of engaging in politics and regaining justice
  • multi-temporal and multi-scalar analyses of new modes of living and liveability in environments damaged by degradation and dispossession

We have plans for a joint publication related to the key topics of this session.

paper.title: Multi-layered harms and struggles for responsibility at frontlines of oil extraction in Mexico
Un/making futures: Rivers, ruination, and the multi-temporalities of repair in Cambodia
Repairing the desert? The use of desalinated water in mining in the context of green transition in Chile
The Politics of Repair: Responsibility
Corporate Narratives of Harm & the Abdication of Accountability in the Extractive Industries

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Movements, memories and moments of democratic localism (1)
schedule_event.description:
Localist experiments have a long, varied and ambivalent lineage (Beveridge and Cochrane 2023), connecting from the escapist commune, through different municipalist waves including the Greater London Council and Barcelona en Comu, to antagonistic and alternative provision practices of parallel institutions, such as public pharmacies (Gonnet et al, 2025), solidarity economies (Arampatzi 2022) and urban agriculture (Traill 2023). The new municipalist moment, apparently stalled and deemed in crisis (Roth et al, 2023), may be the loudest recent iteration of this. However, when contextualised in a broader swathe of more ordinary municipalisations across the globe (Cumbers and Paul 2020), the persistence of local practices of mutualism, experimentation and self-organisation becomes apparent; often operating within as well as outwith formal policy processes.

In this session, we invite papers that attend to the persistence of what we term "democratic localism": an umbrella term we use to denote a global and often interlinked paradigm of locally-scaled political interventions, both in, against and beyond the state, that reimagine everyday life from the ground up. Often such projects draw inspiration and elements of practice from a global elsewhere, with a circulation of policies akin to the movement of policy – but driven by a more heterodox set of actors, less a set of jet-setting policy elite and more rooted in everyday action and activism. Such a hypothesized movement (loosely conceived) draws on social and professional networks, histories of activism and the reification of iconic places like Barcelona or Rojava as metonyms for a set of often participatory practices that are under-explored.

Whilst the current political moment is increasingly framed by rising authoritarianisms and entrenched crises, these movements persist. How they do so, in inhospitable times, remains a provocative question.

paper.title: Alternatives from the Margins: Between Cooptation, Survival, and Social Change in Lebanon
Advancing Democratic Localism: inhabitance, the politics of place and radical municipalism in Scotland and beyond
The genealogies and ‘afterlives’ of progressive municipalism: The Greater London Council and Burlington’s municipalist experiments in comparative perspective
Self-organisation and relational governance in transient urban socio-cultural spaces: action  pathways for cultural democratic agency
Conjunctural Urbanisms: Mobilising strategies for post-growth statecraft

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Food on the move (1): geographies of dietary change and (re)valuation
schedule_event.description: Efforts to improve the sustainability and resilience of Western food systems will ultimately entail eating differently. Whether adopting new food sources, refraining from others, or returning to earlier ways of eating, dietary change of some sort appears unavoidable. This raises important questions for those seeking to improve Western food systems. How do things become appreciated and popularised as food? How, in fact, do things become regarded as food in the first place? Food geographers have made important contributions towards answering such questions, particularly across topics such as production-consumption linkages, ‘alternative’ ways of organising and producing food, and explorations of how food (and food waste) are enacted and categorised in practice. However, there is still much to learn about how foods circulate and become (re)valued within different geographies of production and consumption, knowledge around which is essential if we are to organise Western food systems differently. This session aims to advance debates in these areas. It asks: how do foods move around, both physically (e.g. foods travelling to different places) and socioculturally (e.g. foods becoming de-/re-valued by different eaters)? What does this tell us about how Western food systems might be organised in a more just, resilient and sustainable way?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Post-Foundational Geography in a Turbulent and Polarised World
schedule_event.description:
This session seeks to explore how the field of post-foundationalism geographies can attend to an era marked by intensifying turbulence and polarisation. The mid-2020s are increasingly characterised by escalating geopolitical conflicts amidst the fracturing of the post-Cold War order - what Francis Fukuyama now alleges is the ‘return of history’ - alongside proliferating and deepening culture-wars; ever-worsening environmental crises and the conflicts they generate; enduring populist campaigns from the right and resurgent populist gestures from the left; the normalisation of crisis; the consolidation of platform capitalism, AI and data-driven governance, and techno-optimistic narratives; and deepening wealth inequalities. Taken together, the present moment appears marked by profound instability, polarisation, and a sense that these differences are reaching boiling point.

Post-foundational geography is concerned with the contingency, partiality and provisionality of all spatial orders, differentiating between those orders of politics that regulate and delimit the parameters of social possibility, and the ever-present political potential for things to unfold otherwise. Doing so, it foregrounds the tensions between established power relations and alterity, structure and agency, and constraint and possibility. The field has moved beyond earlier preoccupations with post-politics (Dikeç, 2005; Swyngedouw, 2011) to considering the contingencies of social, spatial, and political life, opening conversations with allied fields (Blakey et al., 2022; Landau, Pohl, Roskamm, 2021), and exploring repoliticisation (Blakey, 2024). Consequently, productive exchanges with feminist, environmental, affective, relational, and negative lines of thought are developing (Kapsali, 2020; Kenis, 2025; Landau-Donnelly and Pohl, 2023; Nelson-Owens, 2025).

This session invites contributions that chart, theoretically, empirically, or otherwise, how post-foundational geography’s theoretical toolkit can be applied or adapted to attend to the turbulent and polarised present.

Contributions may consider, but are not limited to:

  • How post-foundational geographies can relate and respond to emergent political challenges that we may not support or agree with;
  • What remains beyond political dispute in a nonetheless volatile world;
  • The contingency of post-foundational praxis itself, and when, how, or whether contingent grounds ought to be defended;
  • The methodological and ethical challenges of researching conflict, polarisation, and inequality from a post-foundational perspective;
  • The role of affect, embodiment, and everyday practices in sustaining or unsettling polarised spatial orders;
  • The limits, failures, or risks of post-foundational approaches in moments of crisis;
  • How post-foundational thought can engage questions of justice without reinstating fixed political foundations.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Politics of valuation: Financial flows in the political geography of resources
schedule_event.description:
Resource extraction is a highly capital-intensive industry, sustained by a variety of financial flows. Whether expressed as equity investment, derivatives, bank loans, capital markets' bonds or public finance instruments (subsidies, guarantees, etc.), these financial flows are not simply movements of capital: they shape the political geography of resources. In fact, they structure power relations (dependence, risk mitigation, governance of uncertainty, control of information, capture of value, etc.) and determine the materialization of extractive activities in space (investment locations, connections between territories, political and environmental governance, local distribution of revenues etc.).

This panel proposes to examine how financial flows and financial instruments shape the political geography of resources. Drawing on constructivist approaches (Raffestin 1980; Bridge 2009), we understand resources as processes of continuous construction at the intersection of material substances, political and economic contexts, technological availability, legal frameworks, but also financial mechanisms and instruments. These mechanisms and instruments operate at different scales and are networked to different places (i.e. financial centers, offshore centers, regional markets etc). Hence construction processes of resources are entangled in financial valuation processes.

This approach to resources places great importance on the notion of value. Drawing on pragmatic approaches to valuation (Hercelin 2025), "the" value of a resource, of an economic project, or of a territory is shaped by the particular rationalities of financial instruments and models (Doganova 2024) - such as expected profitability, commodification, risk management, etc. Such financial valuation practices are contested by other valuation regimes - such as inalienability of land, competiting land use, the sanctity of a substance, etc. Instruments and models themselves can also be employed in or even act as sites of resource conflicts, for example for computing the distribution of mining rents or environmental compensation.

Approaching resources through the lens of financial geography raises methodological challenges. The financial world has proven difficult to access. In this panel, we wish to give space to methodological considerations: on the one hand, how can we access financial data and actors? And on the other hand, how can the tools of financial geography inform research in the political geography of resources?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Reimagining rural mobility: Everyday experiences, practices and opportunities in low-density regions (1)
schedule_event.description:
Rural transport disadvantages are often overlooked in academic and policy debates despite increasing evidence of inequalities linked to rural living, such as participation in education, labour and social life, as well as access to health. The majority of households have at least one car in rural areas of high-income economies such as the UK and a car is seen as essential to meet basic daily needs (Smith et al., 2012). Nevertheless, there are significant groups of people in rural areas who do not have car access (Chatterjee et al, 2019) or struggle financially to run a car (Mattioli et al, 2018). There is limited knowledge of the mobility experiences and practices of these groups. Buses are the main alternative for rural residents without a car, but the withdrawal of bus services in the UK risks increasing car dependence. There is strong interest currently in whether emerging mobility options such as demand responsive transport, car sharing and e-bikes can fill the gap and transform rural mobility.

These double sessions on rural mobility explore rural mobilities through empirical evidence from around the world, including:

  • Everyday mobility practices and coping strategies in low-density contexts, such as lift-giving, informal carpooling, and mobility rationing
  • The consequences of limited mobility for access to education, employment, healthcare, care responsibilities, and social participation
  • The intersectional dimensions of rural mobility disadvantage, including gender, age, disability, income, ethnicity, migration status, and caring roles
  • The relationship between transport disadvantage, deprivation, poverty, and spatial inequality
  • Longitudinal changes in mobility in response to policy interventions, such as bus service withdrawal or the introduction of demand-responsive transport
  • Community-led, informal, or alternative mobility solutions and their limitations
  • Alternative visions for the future of rural mobility

paper.title: Understanding Trasport Disadvantage and User Experiences of Demand Responsive Transport in Rural Areas of England

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Territories, places, and practices of freedom in transatlantic perspective
schedule_event.description: If ‘freedom is a place’ (Gilmore, 2022), what are the geographies of its social relations (Massey, 1994)? How might freedom be understood not only as a location, but also as a practice, a process, or a mode of inhabiting the world? How can we bring together a ‘progressive sense of place’, as ‘articulated moments in networks of social relations and understandings’ (Massey, 1994), with territorialised projects of self-liberation and the aesthetic representation of freedom? In this session, we seek to place the lived spaces — and the conceptual, aesthetic and cosmopolitical practices — of the quilombo, the palenque, and the maroon community into conversation with wider projects of abolition, histories of the commune, and dreams of utopia. Interrogating the political and philosophical commitments, lived realities, and aesthetic politics of territories of freedom in the Atlantic world, we approach them not only as fixed or bounded formations, but also as provisional, mobile, and relational forms of inhabitation (Glissant, 2009; Harney & Moten, 2014). This session seeks to open up a conversation about the spatial and representational dimensions of freedom in comparative and transatlantic perspective.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Spaces of Inequalities: Using Insights from Geography to Understand Contemporary Educational Inequalities
schedule_event.description:
Research on educational inequalities has deep historical roots and spans areas such as unequal access to educational institutions and stratification in educational transitions. Such research has also examined varied prospects for navigating norms within educational institutions, as well as other reproductive boundaries related to class, ethnicity, gender, and migration. Indeed, inequality remains a significant concern for scholars worldwide, as gaps between groups persist and, in some areas, steadily increase, especially in new geographies of gentrification and globalization. Further, some patterns of inequality, such as those relating to social class, have become more entrenched over time, while others emerge and disappear, as struggles over social, material, and symbolic goods, as well as political decision-making, change and take new shapes (see van Zanten 2009; Zancajo & Bonal 2020; Yoon et al. 2025). In this proposed panel, we focus on emerging spaces of inequalities within sociological and geographical research on education at local and global scales.

This panel will bring together an international group of scholars with expertise in educational mobility, segregation, choice, and marketization, as these have become key areas in contemporary educational change, reform, and policy-making, shaping inequalities. Focus will range from structural-level analysis to specific groups (e.g., elites, middle class, working class, racial and ethnic minorities). Further, topics will include current developments, such as global trends of student mobility, education marketization across various educational sectors, changing politics of access and educational policies that affect precarious groups, changing societal conditions and increasing structural inequalities, and emerging socio-spatial configurations of urban contexts contributing to educational inequalities. This panel will also explore where policymakers and institutional decision-makers have endeavoured to create greater inclusion for historically underrepresented groups. In doing so, our goal is to highlight the significance of place and space in the intersections of (re)production and resistance to educational inequalities across several sites included in this panel.

Four panellists will elaborate on the changing geographies of inequalities and how they are intertwined with educational inequalities. This geographical emphasis will provide new insights into current debates and discussions on educational disparities at local and global scales, as well as their interrelated connections. A panellist from England will share her research on student mobility opportunities and inequalities in post-Brexit. Another panellist will focus on how school types, racial composition, and patterns of white enrollment have changed across major U.S. cities since 2000 to assess how gentrification shapes educational inequalities. Lastly, two panellists will share their collaborative research on mixed-methods approaches to provide a more nuanced understanding of the changing geographies of increasingly unequal programs of choice offered in public (i.e., state) schools in Canada. All panellists will be invited to reflect further on the challenges and contributions of their chosen research approaches, both to the object of study and to the broader field of socio-spatial research on educational inequalities. We believe this year’s conference theme aligns well with our panel’s focus on studying the inequalities of spaces, with an emphasis on education, which has become ever more critical to shaping long-term social inequalities.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Understanding Neighbourhoods from the Global South: Inside-Out Perspectives on Sustainable, Healthy and Learning Cities (1)
schedule_event.description:
Urban neighbourhoods – often the primary scale at which residents experience, organise and negotiate urban life – are increasingly recognised as critical sites for advancing sustainability, health and learning agendas. However, dominant theories, datasets and policy frameworks in neighbourhood studies are largely shaped by Global North perspectives (Baffoe and Kintrea, 2023), often adopting top-down approaches and externally defined models of urban development (UN-Habitat, 2014). These frameworks often fail to capture the socio-spatial complexities, informal systems and context-dependent everyday practices that characterise many neighbourhoods in the Global South (Parnell and Robinson, 2012).

This session advances an inside-out approach to neighbourhood studies in the Global South, highlighting residents' lived experiences, everyday practices and locally embedded knowledge systems. It builds on emerging comparative, place-based and co-produced approaches to understanding neighbourhoods (Lees et al., 2016), as well as neighbourhood-scale analysis from the Sustainable, Healthy and Learning Cities and Neighbourhoods project (Wang et al., 2025). We explore how neighbourhoods are planned, designed and managed under conditions of rapid urbanisation, informality, socio-economic inequality, and institutional complexity.

We invite papers that critically examine planning approaches, urban design practices, and neighbourhood management and governance practices across cities in the Global South. The session welcomes contributions from scholars at all career stages and encourages submissions from researchers and practitioners working in, or in close collaboration with, communities in the Global South. Contributions may examine formal and informal planning systems, community-led and hybrid governance arrangements, neighbourhood design and public space, neighbourhood sustainability practices, service provision, mobility, health and learning environment, and data-driven neighbourhood interventions. We also welcome methodological papers that have used advanced quantitative or qualitative research approaches in examining neighbourhoods in the Global South.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Understanding the Spatial Inequalities of Health and Wellbeing in the North East of England: Local Authority Perspectives (1)
schedule_event.description:
Health inequalities are widely understood and felt at a geographical level. The wider determinants of health - or the building blocks of health - are intrinsically linked to the qualities of the places in which our communities live, work and access services. Local authorities are uniquely positioned to see and respond to these spatial inequalities at both local and hyper-local levels.

Evidence-informed practice and decision-making within Local Authorities has been strengthened by a growing network of research infrastructure, including NIHR Health Determinants Research Collaborations (HDRCs), Local Authority Research Practitioners (LARPs), and support services such as the Research Support Service (RSS). Understanding how this growing practice is being used to explore, understand and address spatial inequalities in health and wellbeing remains a critical challenge.

Health inequalities in the North East of England are worse and widening compared with the rest of England (Corris, V. et al., 2020). Tackling these inequalities is a shared priority for all Local Authorities in the region and an important focus for applied, place-based research.

This panel session brings together those doing research in this area across local authorities, voluntary, community, and social enterprise (VCSE) organisations, and academic partners, to share the work they have been doing and the challenges they face.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The social reproduction of platform economies (1)
schedule_event.description:
The social reproduction of platform economies

Organisers: Sam Nowak (Durham University) and Carolyn Prouse (Queen’s University)

Amidst the ongoing crisis of care and in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, there has been a resurgence of interest in the geographies, infrastructures and labour of social reproduction (Rodríguez-Rocha, 2021; Prouse, 2025). In parallel, geographers have highlighted how all manner of social processes are now increasingly intermediated through digital platforms, powered by big data, and shaped by algorithms (Langley and Leyshon, 2017). This session invites papers concerned with these converging trends, exploring the social reproduction of platform economies.

While it has become axiomatic that platform firms generate value through interlinked processes of data capture (Langley and Leyshon, 2017), labour exploitation (van Doorn and Badger, 2020), and rent extraction (Sadowski, 2020), we suggest that thinking through social reproduction helps move beyond universalisations of ‘the platform’ to consider the everyday practices, social infrastructures, and racialised and gendered labour regimes that underpin, and are reproduced through, platform economies. Recent scholarship has highlighted the shortfalls of universalising and economistic theories of the platform through a focus on: how on-demand domestic work economies sometimes fail (Dattani, 2021); the marketisation, management, and monitoring of domestic space through various forms of ‘caring’ surveillance (Sadowski, Strengers & Kennedy, 2021); the differential encoding of bodies for access to food and other means of social reproduction (Akbari, 2025); the collectivisation practices of urban gig workers to manage occupational risk (Kwan, 2022; Nowak, 2023); and the variegated gendered effects/affects of platform work and digital economies (James, 2024; Hardcastle & Loomis, 2025).

In this session we build on this work to ask: How are digital technologies and platform monopolies re-configuring communities of care, mutual aid labor, and infrastructures of social reproduction? What conceptualisations of social reproduction are useful at this moment to reveal the extractive nature of digital capitalism and the re-making of vulnerability/precarity/life/care? How might a focus on ‘life’s work’ reshape how we think about the digitally-mediated city and urban life?

This session invites papers that seek to answer these questions. We are interested in work that includes, but is not limited to:

  • The reconfiguration of urban spaces and processes of social reproduction through platform and gig economy work;
  • The (re)production of gendered, racialised, classed, migrant and caste precarity through platform economies;
  • Dependence on Big Tech infrastructure for everyday social reproduction, and/or the collectivised contestations of this dependence;
  • Glitch politics and digital infrastructural failure
  • The relationship between social reproduction, the digital commons, and enclosure
  • The affective allure of predictive analytics, digital surveillance, and genAI in everyday spaces of social reproduction
  • The platformisation of care and domestic work
  • The platformisation of housing, education, and health systems and technologies
  • Socio-technical networks of mutual aid, platform cooperatives, and/or social movements that make life in the shadow of fascism
  • Digital and platform technologies of biological reproduction, including femtech and reprotech

paper.title: Delivering and caring from the driver’s seat: automobility and social reproduction in platform food delivery
Between calculability and absences: The platformisation of paid domestic work in Santiago de Chile
Deregulating the City: Platform work, food cultures, and the shifting urban landscape in Bangalore
Platformised domestic labour

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Negotiating Dignity: Rethinking Justice in Geographical Research (2)
schedule_event.description:
Papers in this session place dignity at the centre of geographical research on justice. Over the past two decades, justice has become a core concern across multiple subdisciplines, including critical urban geography, environmental and climate justice, feminist and care geographies, Black and anti-racist geographies, political geography, and mobilities and transport geography. Much of this work has productively moved beyond distributive frameworks to address recognition, participation, embodiment, affect, and everyday power (Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). Yet the normative and lived stakes of these debates are often articulated indirectly. Dignity helps name what is frequently at issue but insufficiently theorised: the ability to inhabit space without humiliation, erasure, or disposability, and to exercise autonomy and social standing in the face of structural inequality. At the same time, dignity is a concept with a complex and contested history. Its universalist claims—long embedded in law, bioethics, and humanitarian governance—have often failed to confront colonial, racialised, gendered, and caste-based hierarchies, and at times have actively reproduced them (Misztal, 2013). Recent geographical scholarship has responded by advancing relational, contextual, and politically grounded understandings of dignity, attentive to how it is produced, denied, and negotiated through everyday encounters with institutions, infrastructures, and publics (Grossmann and Trubina, 2022; Kariũki and Martens, 2026; Plyushteva, 2023; McIlwaine et al., 2025). Taken together, dignity-oriented research extends geographical debates on justice. It draws attention to the affective and ethical dimensions of inequality that are often muted in distributive or procedural accounts. In doing so, it highlights how injustice is reproduced through routine practices of degradation and erasure.

References

Grossmann, K., & Trubina, E. (2022). Dignity in urban geography: Starting a conversation. Dialogues in Human Geography, 12(3), 406-426.

Hopkins, P. (2021). Social geography III: Committing to social justice. Progress in Human Geography, 45(2), 382-393.

Kariũki, W., & Martens, K. (2026). Dignified transport: The case of paratransit. Travel Behaviour and Society, 42, 101140.

McIlwaine, C., Peppl, R., & Cal Angrisani, C. (2025). Reclaiming dignity through creative encounters among Brazilian women survivors of violence in London. cultural geographies, 14744740251319039.

Misztal, B. A. (2013). The idea of dignity: Its modern significance. European Journal of Social Theory, 16(1), 101-121.

Plyushteva, A. (2023). Negotiating dignity in public geography: The ethics of public engagement in pandemic times. Area, 55(1), 53-61.

Przybylinski, S. (2022). Where is justice in geography? A review of justice theorizing in the discipline. Geography Compass, 16(3), e12615.
