schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Researching the Digitalising State (1)
schedule_event.description: Across the world, the state is undergoing deep, fundamental shifts in how it produces data, manages information and digitises bureaucratic workflows. The everyday work of the state – to govern - is now enmeshed with technological appendages that have created what Sandra Braman so eloquently calls a ‘Change of State’ (Braman 2006). There is now a range of complex forms of ‘stateness’ (Jones 2002) arising from the last few decades of state restructuring, accelerated by the proliferation of digital/geospatial platforms and the increasing presence of private-sector actors within state offices. While these developments promise efficiency, transparency, and accountability in the state, they also reconfigure state power in ways that are often opaque, uneven, and difficult for critical geographers to study (Datta and Reyes Carranza 2026). This panel will bring together methodological contributions that interrogate the ‘digitalising state’ (Datta 2023) – a dynamic assemblage of state power across spaces, scales and actors, across public, private and multilateral organisations, across paper and digital information infrastructures, and across political-technological terrains of data, code and platforms. While the state has been notoriously hard to research, there have been significant contributions to investigating the ‘everyday state’ (Hull 2012; Gupta 2012; Dunleavy et al. 2006) from anthropology and organisation studies. What might critical geographers learn from these approaches, and how might we deploy a geographical lens to studying the digitalising state?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Author meets critics: Hanno Brankamp's Occupied Refuge: Humanitarian Colonization and the Camp in Kenya
schedule_event.description: This author-meets-critics session addresses  Occupied Refuge: Humanitarian Colonization and the Camp in Kenya (Duke University Press: 2026) by Hanno Brankamp. Drawing on rich and original ethnographic research in the Kakuma refugee camp in Kenya, this book provides insight into the much broader and persistent political project of humanitarian colonization, and the racialized violence of refuge. Panelists Claudio Minca, Martina Tazzioli, and Yolanda Weima will discuss the book. Following a response by the author, time will also be allotted to audience questions and further exchange.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Author meets critics: Sam Halvorsen’s Territorializing Democracy: Strategies of Popular Participation in Buenos Aires
schedule_event.description:
Scholars are invited to share critical reflections on Sam Halvorsen’s book Territorializing Democracy: Strategies of Popular Participation in Buenos Aires (University of Georgia Press).

Territorializing Democracy argues that the political control of space is key to understanding political participation in the city. Drawing on a decade of research in Buenos Aires, this book shows that participation is not simply a response to innovations in urban governance; it is a strategy rooted in the relational context of territory. Examining key sites of activism over recent years—campaigning for a people’s park, upgrading an informal settlement, the “national-popular” movement of Kirchnerism, and struggles over urban redevelopment—the analysis contributes to pressing democratic debates around autonomy and self-management, populism and clientelism, and democratic innovation and “participatory articulations.” Through the lens of space and geography, this book offers a relational analysis of popular participation, working between multiple neighborhoods and scales, across different struggles, and between the streets and political institutions, activists, and politicians. In doing so, Halvorsen proposes a dual understanding of the territorialization of democracy: a reflection of the changing political conditions shaping cities today and a tool for assessing how democratic practices emerge from specific, grounded struggles over space.

Discussants:

  • Simone Veglio (Malmo University)
  • Yu-Shan Tseng (University of Southampton)
  • Fiona Anciano (University of Western Cape)

Chair: Cristina Temenos (University of Manchester)

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Power, Class and Politics in Green and Blue Spaces: Geographies of Dissonance and Resistance in Leisure and Recreational Activities (1)
schedule_event.description:
Contemporary engagements with nature, whether in forests, coastlines, rivers, parks, or urban green corridors, are highly political. Leisure and recreation in green and blue spaces are often celebrated as places of wellbeing, connection, and sustainability. However, they are also spaces where power, exclusion, and class relations are reinforced. This session explores how practices such as walking, swimming, surfing, fishing, boating, climbing, camping and community gardening reveal tensions between access and enclosure, tradition and innovation, privilege and marginalisation (Aquino et al., 2022).

We aim to highlight the dissonance that arises when social inequalities intersect with leisure in natural environments, and to analyse the resistances carried out by communities that challenge dominant narratives about who belongs in these spaces and how these spaces should be used (Bates & Moles, 2022; Wheaton et al., 2019). Contributions might explore themes such as the commodification of recreational landscapes; exclusions from blue- and green-leisure; policing and surveillance of public nature spaces; disputes over conservation and “rewilding”; or grassroots movements reclaiming access and reimagining outdoor practices.

By focusing on power, class, and politics in discussions of leisure, this session encourages critical reflection on how green and blue spaces are governed, contested, and transformed (O’Connor et al., 2022) within the wider context of the Anthropocene (Clark & Szerszynski, 2021). We invite interdisciplinary contributions from geography, sociology, cultural studies, political ecology, and related fields that showcase empirical, theoretical, and methodological innovations for examining dissonance and resistance within leisure and recreational life (Olive & Wheaton, 2020; Waitt et al., 2009).

References

Aquino, K., Wise, A., Velayutham, S., Parry, K. D., & Neal, S. (2022). The right to the city: Outdoor informal sport and urban belonging in multicultural spaces. Annals of Leisure Research, 25(4), 472–490.

Bates, C., & Moles, K. (2022). Bobbing in the park: wild swimming, conviviality and belonging. Leisure Studies, 43(6), 887–899. https://doi.org/10.1080/02614367.2022.2085774

Clark, N. and Szerszynski, B. (2021). Planetary Social Thought: The Anthropocene Challenge for the Social Sciences. Cambridge: Polity.

O’Connor, P., Evers, C., Glenney, B., & Willing, I. (2022). Skateboarding in the Anthropocene: Grey spaces of polluted leisure. Leisure Studies, 42(6), 897–907. https://doi.org/10.1080/02614367.2022.2153906

Olive, R., & Wheaton, B. (2020). Understanding Blue Spaces: Sport, Bodies, Wellbeing, and the Sea. Journal of Sport and Social Issues, 45(1), 3–19. https://doi.org/10.1177/0193723520950549

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Power, Class and Politics in Green and Blue Spaces: Geographies of Dissonance and Resistance in Leisure and Recreational Activities (2)
schedule_event.description:
Contemporary engagements with nature, whether in forests, coastlines, rivers, parks, or urban green corridors, are highly political. Leisure and recreation in green and blue spaces are often celebrated as places of wellbeing, connection, and sustainability. However, they are also spaces where power, exclusion, and class relations are reinforced. This session explores how practices such as walking, swimming, surfing, fishing, boating, climbing, camping and community gardening reveal tensions between access and enclosure, tradition and innovation, privilege and marginalisation (Aquino et al., 2022).

We aim to highlight the dissonance that arises when social inequalities intersect with leisure in natural environments, and to analyse the resistances carried out by communities that challenge dominant narratives about who belongs in these spaces and how these spaces should be used (Bates & Moles, 2022; Wheaton et al., 2019). Contributions might explore themes such as the commodification of recreational landscapes; exclusions from blue and green leisure; policing and surveillance of public nature spaces; disputes over conservation and “rewilding”; or grassroots movements reclaiming access and reimagining outdoor practices.

By focusing on power, class, and politics in discussions of leisure, this session encourages critical reflection on how green and blue spaces are governed, contested, and transformed (O’Connor et al., 2022) within the wider context of the Anthropocene (Clark & Szerszynski, 2021). We invite interdisciplinary contributions from geography, sociology, cultural studies, political ecology, and related fields that showcase empirical, theoretical, and methodological innovations for examining dissonance and resistance within leisure and recreational life (Olive & Wheaton, 2020; Waitt et al., 2009).

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Visualising the city: Urban imaginaries and aesthetics in a digital world (1)
schedule_event.description:
Urban imaginaries (Lindner & Meissner, 2019) play a key role in framing aspirations in planning, branding, infrastructure and movements for change within urban settings. Familiar tropes such as ‘world class city’, ‘eco city’, ‘smart city’, ‘clean city’ are underpinned by the circulation of aesthetic forms expressing achievement, desire and possibility, or demanding accountability when these are found wanting. 

In the last two decades, with increased access to technologies such as smart phones and social media platforms, urban imaginaries have been digitised (Degen & Rose, 2024). The contemporary urban imaginary is rendered through a plethora of multimodal digital media such as selfies, memes, reels and AI generated content. Visualising the city is no longer confined to political authorities, planning experts or speculative developers and now includes anyone (residents, netizens and tourists alike) with access to the internet and the means to produce digital media.We invite papers that explore these aesthetic forms of the urban imaginary with suggested themes including, but not limited to

  • Image making practices by citizen led initiatives
  • The urban imaginary in social media (possibility, accountability, nostalgia)
  • Intersectional urban imaginaries
  • AI generated urban imaginaries
  • Image making practices by governments
  • Aesthetic imaginaries of speculative developments

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Ambiguous Geographies of Digital Dis/Connection
schedule_event.description: In this session, we invite speakers to start from the provocation that relations of digital dis/connection are necessarily ambiguous. Digital technologies are now central to how people interact with the state, access welfare, navigate borders, and maintain everyday life more generally. Technologies such as the smartphone (or other ‘smart’ devices, platforms, or apps) are often presented as tools of connection: making services more efficient, improving communication, and increasing access to support. However, digital systems are rarely experienced as simply connective or helpful; especially for those governed through complex political systems such as asylum seekers, refugees, irregular migrants, welfare claimants, or those living through war and genocide. Negotiating these tensions, our speakers each explore how ambiguity becomes a (conceptual, methodological, practical) tool of doing geographical research at the intersection of dis/connection.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geodatafication: Socio-technical Infrastructures, Economies and (Geo-)Politics of Datafied Space (1)
schedule_event.description:
The digital transformation is fundamentally spatial. Contemporary forms of digital capitalism, platform economies, and data-driven governance depend on the continuous translation of space, spatial relations, and territorial knowledge into machine-readable geospatial data. At the same time, geospatial data are a strategic resource in geopolitical competition, territorial surveillance, and contemporary warfare. From real-time logistics and location-based services to satellite infrastructures, drone operations, border surveillance, and military targeting, geodatafication lies at the heart of how spatial power is exercised, contested, and operationalized in the digital age.

While recent debates in geography and the social sciences have addressed datafication, platformization, digital infrastructures, and geopolitics, the specific role of geospatial data and its infrastructures remains under-explored and under-theorized. This is particularly striking given that geospatial data are central to

  • the coordination and optimization of circulation, logistics, and value chains in digital capitalism,
  • the territorialization of digital platforms and markets,
  • the automation of surveillance, border regimes, and predictive governance,
  • the conduct of contemporary wars, hybrid conflicts, and geopolitical struggles over sovereignty and territory,
  • and the practices of digital humanitarianism through open and collaborative geospatial data.

Therefore this session foregrounds geodatafication as a foundational process of the digital transformation. We conceptualize geodatafication as the socio-technical translation of space and spatial relations into geospatial data, enabling the calculability, governability, and operationalization of territory, circulation, and mobility. Geodatafication not only allows digital information to be anchored in physical space; it also provides the infrastructural, economic, and political basis for the spatial logistics of digital capitalism and for emerging forms of geopolitical control, surveillance, and warfare.

The session invites papers that critically examine the political, economic, infrastructural, and everyday dimensions of geodatafication, with particular attention to its dual role in locating digital capitalism and operationalizing geopolitics and conflict.

We welcome contributions that engage with, but are not limited to, the following themes:

  • Geodatafication and the spatial logics of digital capitalism (platforms, logistics, circulation, labor)
  • Geospatial data as infrastructure of territorial control, surveillance, and security
  • Geodatafication, geopolitics, and geo-economic competition
  • Open geospatial data, digital commons, and their incorporation into geopolitical, humanitarian and commercial strategies
  • Satellite systems, positioning infrastructures (GPS, Galileo), and geospatial data sovereignty
  • Geodatafication, GeoAI, and automation in military, security, and border regimes
  • Mapping, targeting, and geospatial intelligence in contemporary warfare and hybrid conflicts
  • Dual-use geospatial technologies and civil–military entanglements
  • Geodatafication, human rights documentation, and counter-mapping in conflict zones
  • Infrastructures, standards, and formats of geospatial data as sites of power and contestation

The session welcomes conceptual, theoretical, and empirical contributions from across human geography and allied disciplines, including critical GIS, digital geography, political economy, political geography, STS, critical infrastructure studies, and geopolitics. Contributions may draw on qualitative, quantitative, mixed, or experimental methods.

By bringing together perspectives on digital capitalism and geopolitics through the lens of geodatafication, this session aims to advance a more integrated understanding of how datafied spatial infrastructures shape contemporary power relations—from everyday platform practices to territorial surveillance and war.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Housing, Population Change, and Spatial Inequality (2)
schedule_event.description: Housing is a central area through which social and spatial inequalities are (re)produced and experienced. Processes of population change, including migration, ageing, and urban growth, are deeply intertwined with housing systems. schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Food transition pathways, politics of feeling and the geographies of inequality
schedule_event.description:
Cultural political geographers Anderson and Secor (2025) describe a new politics of feeling – one where the increasing influence of populism in socio-political discourse, runs alongside progressive and neoliberal politics. This session examines how these political affects are carrying agency in various ways across the food system, specifically in terms of what the food system should deliver for all of us (human and non-human), including those in positions of inequity, poverty and precarity.

The UK Government has ambitions for a healthy, more affordable, sustainable and resilient food system. The European Commission has a vision for agriculture and food that fosters trust and dialogue across the entire value chain, prioritising food security and sovereignty. These ambitions are politically strategic as they attempt to find meaningful engagement, if not agreement, about the how across all aspects of the food system. And yet the populous is a heterogeneous beast that mobilises politically in incoherent, cross-cutting and divergent ways within the food system. We have seen, for example, how the cost-of-living crisis is pushing those struggling to afford their food shopping baskets to protest against mainstream political parties. Knife-edge farming businesses feel ignored as not all farmers are alike in terms of resources, assets and business model. (Seasonal) labour challenges across the food sector are attracting political attention, and with that further shifts towards automation.

Such mobilisations signify a ‘new food politics’ (Maye et al 2025) that infuses into everyday life, involving producers, eaters, citizens, carers, workers, politicians, activists and virtual platforms (social media). This politics intersects with feelings of nationalism, identity, community, optimism, care, despair, hope, etc. It challenges norms, consensus frameworks and re-politicises political decision-making and social democracy. The affects of these contemporary ‘political atmospheres’ in turn reshape present and future everyday geographies of food, justice and care.

The session invites contributors to engage critically in this new food politics and in particular with how the emergent geographies of political atmospheres, found variously across diverse communities, are shaping the contours of food system transformation pathways. We welcome empirical, conceptual and methodological papers.

Below is an indicative list of topics:

  • Affective politics of food justice and geographies of inequality
  • Crisis, resilience, atmospheres and new politics of optimism and recognition
  • Just food futures and the voices that need to be re/presented
  • Redefining food system resilience through politics and affect
  • Food system visioning and nationalism in new political atmospheres
  • Justice and political perspectives of food system actors
  • Impacts of populism on consensus frameworks and deliberative democracy
  • The intersection between progressivism, neoliberalism and populism in the politics and geography of food system transformation
  • Geographies of protest, symbolic and soft power and links to food and politics
  • Cases and voices from everyday food geographies: eaters, workers, farmers, carers, citizens, non-humans
  • Perceptions of politics, food-making and justice from rural and urban communities
  • Metabolic food politics and intersections with political ideologies and place-making
  • Which places, nations and regions get left behind in new political atmospheres but equally how do communities respond and provide comfort?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Artificial Intelligence and its Fascist Geographies
schedule_event.description:
As we write this abstract, a standoff between tech giant Anthropic and the US government has made very stark the stakes in an emergent set of formations where technocapitalism and Artificial Intelligence are increasingly the ground for growing fascism globally. In this case, Anthropic has presented itself as the ethical bulwark against the US state’s demand for its AI infrastructure as the backbone for new surveillance, and “fully autonomous weapons”, i.e., arms that will not even require the gamified agencies that drone operators exercise. This pushes us to question the role of AI-led technocracy in circumscribing and attending to neoliberal, authoritarian, and fascist futures.

Tech companies are far from reliable ethical counterweights to fascist regimes. This is evident in the role that Generative AI tech has played in rising authoritarianism globally, not only in the US and the UK, but also in India, Israel, and beyond. These same surveillance, facial recognition, and monitoring technologies increasingly also enact racial capitalist pre-crime regimes, regulate the availability of healthcare, education, and supermarket products, and privatise access to previously public forms of knowledge (i.e. archival material) (Fathallah, 2026; Yang, 2025). Additionally, the datacenters required to power and house such technologies are reproducing fossil fuel energy regimes and exacting new and compounding forms of health and environmental burden on marginalised communities they occupy. Indeed, scholars increasingly argue that with this interpenetration of technologically dense infrastructures of surveillance in everyday life, states across the world are reneging on their social function to instead enact militarised forms of governance (Colón Vargas, 2025, Marie Hampton, 2023). This is the context within which we position our work, which interrogates the relationship between the rapid and often unquestioned normalisation of Generative AI in social and political life, and the pervasive spread of authoritarian and fascist tendencies among states globally.

Specifically, in this panel, we ask how neoliberal regimes, driven by the protection of private capital interests over social democracy have led to AI-led technocratic governance. We ask how Artifical Intelligence has intensified racial capitalist and settler colonial regimes of land dispossession, surveillance, carcerality, and genocide across landscapes in Europe, North America, and the Global South. We are interested in the gendered, racialised, and class politics of Generative AI, and the normalised violence it enables in its everyday use.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Human, Unevenly: Rethinking Human(ist) Geographies of Embodiment, Difference, and Everyday Inequality (1): Embodied Difference and Uneven Humanity
schedule_event.description:
Humanism is often treated in contemporary geography as something already overcome, yet much research continues to rely on assumptions about embodied subjects, situated meaning-making, and ordinary practices of everyday life. This session asks what it means to take that reliance seriously, attending to the uneven afterlives of humanist traditions as they have been critiqued, reshaped, and reworked through feminist, post-colonial, and post-phenomenological interventions. Rather than returning to or rejecting humanism, we invite reflection on how these inheritances persist—quietly and imperfectly—in geographical theory, method, and engagements with inequality across the life course.

The session is particularly interested in research focused on marginal, liminal, and everyday lives: moments of transition, care, exhaustion, precarity, ageing, or maintenance; sites where inequality is felt materially and unevenly; and spaces where justice is negotiated through embodied relations with institutions, infrastructures, environments, and objects. We welcome reflections on methods and practices - whether ethnographic, narrative, participatory, interpretive - that continue to rely on situated interpretation and relationality, even where theoretical vocabularies have shifted.

Rather than calling for a return to classical humanism or a rejection of post-humanist thought, this session opens space to ask how we are already working with the human, unevenly and imperfectly, and with what consequences for feminist, justice-oriented geographical research and debates about just places.

In this session, instead of seeking theoretical resolution or disciplinary closure, we invite contributions that linger with limits: of bodies, of concepts, of methods - or ontologically. In doing so, it asks what kinds of feminist, justice-oriented geographical work become possible when the human is not treated as a problem to be overcome, but as something lived, negotiated, and unevenly shared.

We welcome empirically grounded, conceptually experimental, and methodologically novel contributions exploring:

  • classed and intergenerational belonging, attachment, and place memory;
  • unruly, leaky, or boundary-troubling bodies and their socio-material contexts;
  • intimate inequalities, reproductive geographies, and embodied life-course transitions
  • affective atmospheres, lived precarity, and everyday negotiations of inequality;
  • methodological reflections on

In particular the session welcomes postgraduate and early-career researchers who may wish to share the historical and/or theoretical contexts underpinning their research. The session is intended to provide a relaxed and friendly forum for researchers across and beyond the discipline of geography to share what they are working on with others.

We welcome papers relating to the history of the discipline of geography, the history of geographical knowledge broadly construed, as well as histories and biographies of geographers and geographical thinkers.

We also welcome papers relating to the philosophy of geography, philosophies used within geographical and adjacent disciplines, critical theory broadly construed, the development of geographical theories and the proposal of novel theories within geography and adjacent disciplines, as well as contributions exploring the theoretical contexts underpinning geographical or adjacent research.

The session welcomes cross-, trans-, and multi-disciplinary research that may not straightforwardly or easily fit within the received categories of history and philosophy of geography. In addition, we welcome contributions which emphasise the politics of theory and the relevance of the history and/or philosophy of geography for addressing social, political, and economic challenges.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers Panel: Geographers and 'justice work'
schedule_event.description: This TIBG plenary panel invites exploration of the vital and diverse contributions of geographers whose research, teaching, and praxis are deeply committed to justice work within and beyond the discipline. What is ‘justice work’ and how does it intervene in conditions of ‘injustice’? As a form of labour, how is it is organised, valued, and governed within the academy and beyond? How can ‘justice work’ help us to understand and build towards deeper engagements with justice that go beyond what Don Mitchell (2024) diagnoses as ‘commonsense justice-as-fairness’ which dominates the discipline today? At a moment of intensifying authoritarianism and political backlash against critical scholarship, what are the risks, costs, and constraints of justice work? How do geographers engaged in justice-oriented research, teaching, and public engagement navigate what can be profound risks and ethical stakes, both for scholars and for the communities with whom they work? In opening up these questions for discussion, the panel aims to critically examine justice work as a situated, uneven, and contested form of geographical labour, and to foster collective reflection on how such work might be sustained, protected, and reimagined in increasingly authoritarian political and institutional contexts.

schedule_event.description:
This panel reflects on peoples’ struggles against systems of bordering – from border crossings, detention, and deportation to raids and everyday policing of migrant communities.

Through a focus on radical practices, histories, and geographies of migrant justice and border abolition, this conversation explores the challenges, joys, and benefits of imagining, planning and practicing a world without borders – a world where people have the freedom to move, the freedom to stay, and the freedom to return.

Join this panel session to learn about inspiring research and current debates on migrant justice and border abolition.

Featuring Dr Geoff Boyce (University College Dublin), Dr Martina Tazzioli (University of Bologna), Gracie Mae Bradley (University of Glasgow), Dr Anish Chhibber (Queen Mary University of London), and chaired by Dr Malene Jacobsen (Newcastle University).

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - The State of Geography in HE UK
schedule_event.description: This panel builds on the State of Geography in UK HE report (published February 2026). The report summarises key findings from a snapshot ‘State of Geography’ survey, of all institutions in the UK who offer Higher Education geography degrees. It identified that smaller non-Russell Group institutions are encountering the greatest pressure, large Russell Group institutions in London or the south are expanding, there are uneven student admission changes, and very few institutions have closed their geography programmes but two thirds of geography programmes are reducing the number of optional modules available to students. Many institutions are struggling to retain field class teaching and operating with reduced academic staff numbers, and reduced Professional Service staff and support. A majority of institutions reported increased academic staff workloads, though many are managing to retain research time (albeit in practice research time is shrinking). In this panel we will explore (a) what can we take away from the results that are important for the discipline? and (b) what collective action, response, ways forward are there that will support the future of geography in the UK?

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Towards Just Geography Departments
schedule_event.description:
As a discipline, Geography is underpinned by notions and practices of justice and equity; it seeks to understand and respond to a changing world punctuated by complex and intersecting issues and crises. In recognition that such crises also touch down in the spaces and life worlds of academia, geographers have turned attention to the ‘state’ of the discipline, from the wide-ranging pressures shaping UK Universities to how these are embodied in their workforce through, for example, the rise and normalisation of precarious working conditions (see for example Mason and Megoran, 2021, 2023; Pickerill, 2026; Simpson, 2022; Squire et al., 2025) and the ways in which intersectional factors inform and shape these experiences (see for example Desai, 2017; Evans et al., 2025; Hughes, 2021; Maddrell et al., 2019; Tolia-Kelly, 2017). Such work has prompted critical questions about the future of the discipline (Roast, 2024; Rose-Redwood et al., 2024).

Thinking with such scholarship, this panel pauses with the conference’s theme - Geographies of inequalities: toward just places - by specifically spotlighting the experiences and spaces of the Geography Department. Exploring how questions of justice and injustice (re)make and (re)shape, as well as break, our places of work, it is interested in issues including:

  • Embodied experiences of workplace (in)justice in academia;
  • Everyday and mundane places and spaces of workplace (in)justice;
  • Individual and collective strategies colleagues employ to work toward more just workplace futures;
  • Opportunities for imagining alternative, more just, and equitable workplace futures;
  • The possibilities and impossibilities of justness in the contemporary academy;
  • The effects of workplace (in)justice on the future of geographical research.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Power, inequalities and participation: revisiting food citizenship and beyond (1)
schedule_event.description:
In a food system wrought by crises and inequalities, ‘food citizenship’ emerged alongside ‘food democracy’ as one way of re-politicising food. The concept re-imagines consumers as active citizens who participate in shaping more just food systems. Fundamental questions of participatory justice are at stake: who is able to participate in food governance, on what terms, and with what consequences for social and ecological futures?

Despite its growing traction, limited attention has been paid to intersectionality, situatedness and embodied experiences of ‘food citizenship’, and examining how food system participation is structured by gender, race, class, disability, age and other axes of power. The figure of a ‘citizen’ is never neutral: normative expectations around ‘active citizenship’ are prevalent in food citizenship debates and can reproduce exclusion by privileging particular capacities, temporalities and modes of engagement. Critiques of citizen participation highlight potential to reinforce neoliberal dynamics and subjectivities. Socio-spatial injustices also constrain participation; participation doesn’t inherently ‘fix’ systemic injustices.

Taking food citizenship as an imperfect but productive starting point, this session will critically interrogate the methodologies, geographies and politics of subject formation, participation and justice in the democratisation of food governance across scales and places/territories:

  • Who is (imagined as) a ‘food citizen’? What visions of food citizenship circulate across different spaces and scales, what political work do they perform, and for whom?
  • Who is included or excluded from these imaginaries and practices, and how is power and participation structured through gender, class, race, ethnicity, disability or age?
  • How does embodied food citizenship intersect with food sovereignty, food justice and other debates?
  • Which alternative language, theoretical perspectives or methodological approaches can help reinvigorate participatory justice values and ethical commitments within food citizenship debates and practice?

Four short papers will be followed by a world cafe-style discussion.

paper.title: Rethinking food citizenship through the lens of childhood
Food Democracy in Practice: On the Frictions and Conflicts in Organising Solidarity Kitchens
The making of food citizens as classed and gendered ‘experts by experience’
World-cafe discussion

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Geographies of Reparatory Justice for Historical Enslavement
schedule_event.description:
This session seeks to examine reparatory justice for historical enslavement from explicitly geographical perspectives. As Araujo’s (2017) recent book, Reparations for Slavery and the Slave Trade: A Transnational and Comparative History, makes clear, there has been a long history of claims for reparation for historical enslavement. While highlighting the range and diversity of these claims, the book at the same time reveals further opportunities to examine their historical geographies. The 21st century resurgence of interest in reparatory justice emphasises that the issue is far from in the past. A whole series of popular, academic and political developments, informed by on-going enslaved descendant activism, has brought reparatory justice into the spotlight once more. These range from critiques of celebratory commemorations of abolition (Bressey, 2009) and the Black Lives Matter movement’s protests against racial injustice, to calls for reparatory dialogue and action from governments of nations negatively impacted by European chattel enslavement, notably CARICOM (2014) and Commonwealth leaders in 2024. A spectrum of reparatory justice scholarship exists, examining different meanings and dimensions of reparatory thought (Schwarz, 2022) and action (Beckles, 2013; Shepherd and Reid, 2019), with ‘reparatory history’ practice set out by Hall (2018) as an important strand of reparatory justice. At the academic institutional level, reparatory justice has been supported through the Universities Studying Slavery (USS) consortium established in early 2016, a network with US roots which has expanded more recently to Europe (Beckles 2019; Zacek, 2025).

This session will explore understanding of the ways in which geography matters to reparatory justice, including situated and institutional understandings of reparatory justice past and present, historical geographies of reparatory justice, geographies of reparatory justice in practice and universities and reparatory justice in international context.

Beckles, H M (2013) Britain’s Black Debt: Reparations for Caribbean slavery and native genocide. University of the West Indies Press.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Future-Making, Future-Faking, Future-Breaking? The politics of deception and the power of absurdity around large infrastructure and development projects
schedule_event.description:
Large-scale development and infrastructure projects have historically been driven and shaped through compelling narratives of the future (Anand, Gupta, & Appel, 2018; Larkin, 2018). Promises of growth, competitiveness, national renewal or sustainability are mobilised to secure public consent, unlock finance, and stabilise political coalitions. Yet these futures are often knowingly partial, exaggerated, or impossible to realise (Flyvbjerg, 2007). Rather than failures of planning or implementation, such practices may constitute a normalised political strategy: future-faking, a practice of fixing unfulfilled promises not through real improvements, but instead by an infinite series of new, (absurd) promises. We understand future-faking not simply as misrepresentation, but as a form of power that actively reshapes anticipatory behaviour, reallocates public resources, and produces uneven geographies of risk, hope, and dispossession.

This session examines how actors strategically mobilise infrastructural futures, developmental visions, and grand narratives that they know will not materialise as presented, as well as the political, social, and spatial consequences of doing so, and the ways in which affected people navigate faked futures and resist them. With this, it contributes to the chair’s theme of ‘Geographies of inequality’, and their production through deceptive politics. Further, this session features presentations on both past and current large-scale projects, with an additional focus on exploring the absurd: visions that are as improbable as they are grandiose. Through critical examination, we delve into examples of faked futures; plans that, despite their absurdity, generate tangible and far-reaching material effects.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Legal Animal Geographies: Towards Multispecies Justice in Policy and Practice (1)
schedule_event.description:
There is a vast array of work that explores governance, regulation, and legislation of animals in a range of different spaces, including work examining the role of responsibility in dog walking in the Scottish Outdoor Access Code (Brown and Dilley, 2012; Brown, 2015), regulation of ‘street’ and ‘stray’ dogs in the UK and India (Srinivasan, 2013), the legal blurriness of horseshoe crabs in animal welfare policy (Gorman, 2024), the poorly suited regulations and welfare guidance for fish in UK scientific research (Greenhough et al. 2024), social license to operate in Australian horse racing and horse welfare (Duncan et al., 2018; McManus, 2023), empathy and ethics in the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons Code of Professional Conduct (Donald, 2019), pet’s right to the city in China (Yan, 2025), and the different framings of dog-related policy in the UK (Allen et al., 2025). Bringing these works together raises three key questions:

(1) How can we take the care of animals seriously in current legal systems?(2) How can we contest, challenge, and resist unjust legal systems for animals? (3) How can we create just multispecies worlds? This session explores how multispecies legal justice might be realised.

Taking animals seriously in legal systems requires challenging the anthropocentrism inherent in our understandings of animals as property, tools, and objects (Deckha, 2021; Allen et al., 2025), to instead highlight the ways in which animals’ own subjectivities shape space and everyday life. As Braverman (2016) argues we need to consider the range of ethical and political issues of what it means to govern more-than-human life.

We invite papers that not only highlight current multispecies injustice, but seek to challenge, resist, and contest these systems to create more just multispecies worlds and futures. We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes:

Animals and abuse; Animals and housing; Animals and inequality; Animals and law; Animals and policy (change); Animal campaigning; Animals, history, and policy; Animals, policy, and space; Animals and everyday policing; Human-animal governance; Multispecies justice; Wildlife crime

To submit, please send your paper title and abstract (of up to 250 words) to Daniel Allen (d.allen@keele.ac.uk) and Jamie Arathoon (j.arathoon1@yorksj.ac.uk) by Friday 28th February (This allows session organisers time to review proposals and submit the final session to the RGS-IBG portal by the official deadline of Friday 6th March 2026.)

Please also let us know your contact details, affiliation, and any accessibility requirements.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Living with Urban Inequality: Ageing, Well-being, and Mobility (1)
schedule_event.description:
Urban inequality is lived not only through income disparities or housing outcomes, but through everyday experiences of ageing, care, health, and movement across the city (Lefebvre, 1991; Harvey, 2008). As urban populations age amid widening socio-spatial inequalities, access to mobility, services, social infrastructure, and meaningful participation in urban life becomes increasingly uneven (WHO, 2007). These inequalities are shaped by intersecting relations of class, gender, ethnicity, migration status, and health, and are embedded in neighbourhood change, welfare restructuring, and urban governance (Soja, 2010).

This session brings together urban geographical research that examines how inequality is produced, experienced, and negotiated through the everyday mobilities and immobility of ageing populations. Mobility is understood broadly, encompassing physical movement, access to urban services, social participation, and connections across neighbourhood, city, and transnational scales. The session foregrounds well-being as relational and spatially contingent, shaped by both structural conditions and everyday practices of care, support, and belonging (O’Neil, 2020).

We invite papers that engage empirically and/or theoretically with urban inequality through the lenses of ageing, well-being, and mobility. Relevant themes include but are not limited to:

  • Ageing in unequal urban environments
  • Health, well-being, and spatial inequality
  • Everyday mobilities, immobility, and access to the city
  • Migration, transnational ageing, and urban belonging
  • Gendered, racialised, and classed experiences of later life
  • Care, social infrastructure, and neighbourhood support systems
  • Urban policy, governance, and the uneven provision of care and services
  • Methodological approaches to researching ageing and inequality in cities

The session welcomes qualitative, comparative, and critical contributions that situate lived experience within wider urban processes, contributing to long-standing debates in urban geography around inequality, social reproduction, and uneven development.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Uneven Development: Decolonial, Feminist and Alternative Perspectives (1)
schedule_event.description:
In the face of daunting global challenges – including climate change, biodiversity loss, disruptive technologies, the ascendency of authoritarianism and political cynicism, and geopolitical tensions and conflicts – the orthodox paradigm of development based on growth and modernization, driven by innovation and technological advancement, is increasingly challenged. More and more, the fundamental question is raised whether this model of development is leading us astray, instead of offering a way forward, and is actually an important part of the causes for our current predicament (ss Morales, Evenhuis and Sariego-Kluge, 2025).

This is especially true for the challenge of uneven development and extensive inequities across space. With the orthodox model of development came a belief that the benefits drawn from growth and ongoing modernization will eventually reach the wider society. Yet, this “trickle down” promise has not materialised. Instead, spatial inequalities are widening across the globe along many dimensions (Lobao and Hooks, 2025), starkly visible in many parts of the Global South, and increasingly exposed within the Global North, revealing its multiple “souths”. These inequalities are reshaping political landscapes, fuelling exclusionary and destabilising political movements and harming the progress of sustainable transformations.

Yet, geographers have long shown that the “trickle down” effect is unlikely to occur without intentional intervention (Hudson, 2007; Werner, 2016; Peck, 2019). Moreover, geographical research reveals that “the economy” is not a singular, coherent entity. Rather, it comprises diverse practices, relations and arrangements that coexist and interact across places. Therefore, understanding spatial uneven development requires perspectives that go beyond standard economic metrics. To better understand spatial inequality, and imagine meaningful interventions, we need approaches that reconfigure or expand how we conceptualise the relationship between economy and space.

Current ecological, social, and geopolitical shifts invite a renewed perspective on how economic processes interact with spatial structures and how these interactions contribute to the production and persistence of spatial inequalities. These contexts highlight the need to examine the diverse ways economic activity relates to place, to consider multiple economic logics operating across space, and to explore a wider set of conceptual tools for analysing these dynamics. This includes approaches that foreground feminist, decolonial, ecological, and other alternative understandings of economic life and its spatial expressions. Therefore, we invite papers that:

  • Reimagine the relationship between economy and space through alternative, feminist, decolonial, or other lenses
  • Examine how spatial inequalities are created, reproduced, and transformed from the points of view of decolonial, feminist and alternative approaches
  • Examine plural or non-capitalist economic practices and their spatial implications
  • Offer conceptual, methodological, or empirical insights into how economies operate differently across places
  • Explore the ethical, political, or ecological dimensions of economic activities and propose new frameworks for analysing or intervening in spatial inequality

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Challenging racism in challenging times: geographies of anti-racism (1)
schedule_event.description:
This panel examines the geographies of racism and anti-racism in the context of widening inequalities and ongoing struggles for just places.

Racism remains a deeply spatialised and systemic form of inequality, shaping access to education, health, wellbeing, housing, mobility, safety, and civic participation. Experiences of racism also undermine social inclusion, trust, and senses of belonging, producing unjust places in which equality of opportunity and dignity are unevenly distributed. From neighbourhoods and institutions to online spaces and national imaginaries, racism is embedded in the social, political, and spatial organisation of everyday life.

At the same time, emerging racial justice and dignity frameworks provide new ways of conceptualising and evaluating anti-racism efforts—foregrounding whether people feel that they belong, feel safe and respected in the places they inhabit. These approaches align closely with geographical concerns about place-making, power, and justice.

However, we are currently experiencing challenging times for anti-racism work. Heightened geo-political tensions, political leadership that normalises or sanctions racism and prejudice, and the growing influence of populist and right-wing discourses have reshaped public debate and policy landscapes. New technologies, digital platforms, and online cultures have super charged the spread of racist and extremist attitudes, while strained relationships across cultural groups and with key institutions has weakened allyship and solidarity. Critical, place-based, and action-oriented anti-racism scholarship can make racism more difficult. This will improve community relations, expand citizenship, and build public confidence in diversity and immigration.

This session will examine the challenges facing anti-racism work, while also offering insights into effective, innovative, or hopeful forms of anti-racist action across different spatial, institutional, and political contexts. Collectively, the session aims to advance geographical understandings of racism and anti-racism, and to contribute to debates on how more just, inclusive, and dignified places might be imagined and enacted.

paper.title: Attitudes and behaviours that inform white victimhood narratives, white privilege and claims of anti-white racism
Whiteness and Anti-Asian Racism as Entangled Global Force: Media Discourses in the US, Australia, and Germany
Hoping for Tremors: Doing Anti-Racism Work in the Neoliberal University
Advancing the Measurement of Structural Racism in Housing: A Conceptual Framework
Allyship and the activation of anti-racist geographies: prodding the state into action

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Sand Crisis or Sandthropocene? Geo(morpho)logizing urbanization
schedule_event.description:
The contemporary world is built on sand. It is a foundational material for construction and development, underpinning both land use change and rapid urbanization. A key ingredient of concrete, as well as a granular fill material for land reclamation and soil improvement, sand is geologizing, and geomorphologizing urbanization and economic development  in increasingly dynamic, unpredictable, and disastrous ways (Dawson, 2021; Jamieson, 2024). In recent years, extraction has accelerated so far beyond replacement rates that environmental scientists have posed the possibility of an impending sand crisis and ‘tragedy of the commons’ (Torres et al, 2017). However, geographers have also problematised this  invocation of crisis, whose designation obscures the complexity of sand mining and its relationship to economic development (John, 2021; Lamb, 2023), evident from the recurrence of black markets in sand across the world, which, for such a cheap and voluminous resource, demands further scrutiny.

This session seeks to explore sand in its relationship to urbanization, economic development and the underlying geological and geomorphic processes it is both product and mediator of. The effects of sand mining are keenly felt at the scale of everyday life, foreclosing livelihoods and uprooting ecologies, while its economic role is intimately bound to the most delirious visions of speculative urbanism and state capitalism. It asks whether we are seeing the beginning of a sand crisis, or if we now find ourselves in the Sandthropocene, with sand’s ubiquitous presence in urban and infrastructural form constituting a novel geosocial formation wherein urban and geomorphic systems are rendered interdependent, challenging the terrestrial and geological bias of Anthropocene theory.

paper.title: Minerals, Magic, and Mining: The Politics of Animism in the Age of Critical Minerals
Sands of Destruction
From Riverbed to Real Estate: Sand and the Granular Neoliberal Urban Production in Phnom Penh, Cambodia
Towards a Sandthropocene

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - East and West in Israel/Palestine: Urban Inequalities in Rehabilitation, Demolition, and Infrastructure
schedule_event.description:
The disputed landscape of Israel/Palestine, shaped by modernization and colonial contestation, reveals deep-rooted inequalities embedded in urban planning and spatial design. This session explores the complexities of urban transformation, including planning and infrastructure, which often lead to rehabilitation in some areas and destruction in others. Through diverse spatial analyses by geographers, historians, archaeologists, and urban planners, we investigate the interactions among modernization, urban erasure, urban heritage, and social inequality in cities such as Jerusalem, Jaffa, and Lod/Lid.

In "Defining East: The Rehabilitation of East Jerusalem before and after 1967," Noah Hysler Rubin and Abigail Jacobson examine the role of the East Jerusalem Development Society (EJDS) in rehabilitating inner-city neighborhoods before and after the 1967 war. They analyze urban planners' priorities regarding reconstruction efforts and the definition of urban heritage, and challenge traditional definitions of East and West Jerusalem.

In "From East to East: Displacement, Housing, and the Spatial Reorganization of Tel Aviv-Jaffa," Elia Etkin presents the process of large-scale housing construction initiated by the Halamish company in Jaffa during the 1960s. She situates the development of new housing within broader urban renewal policies while also highlighting residents' resistance to urban clearance and the social ramifications of displacement.

In "Urban Erasure and Unequal Development: Planning, Demolition, and Spatial Inequality in Lod / Lid," Tawfiq Da'adli analyzes how modern urban planning in Lod has perpetuated spatial inequality and urban erasure following the Nakba. His paper reveals how post-1948 planning practices have reshaped the city through processes of demolition and redevelopment, resulting in a fragmented urban landscape marked by disparities among Palestinian and Jewish communities.

Finally, in "Cumulative Infrastructural Injustice: The Longue Durée of Telecommunications in Palestine/Israel," Shira Wilkof traces the development of telecommunication infrastructure in urban and rural Palestine/Israel from British colonial rule to contemporary Israel. She demonstrates how modern infrastructural systems have materially organized the dynamics of colonialism, sovereignty, and contested citizenship, offering the concept of "cumulative infrastructural injustice" as a crucial research paradigm for studying diverse mechanisms of exclusion.

Together, the papers in this session engage with the emergence of urban inequalities through modern planning, urging an exploration of the power relations guiding allegedly mundane planning and infrastructure in the context of Israel/Palestine.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Beyond Access: Lived Experience and Disability Justice in Tourism and Leisure Spaces (1)
schedule_event.description:
Tourism provides significant potential for well-being, social connection, and leisure engagement. However, these benefits are not equally distributed or accessible across the population. This session moves “beyond access” to centre the rich diversity of lived experience and the transformative framework of disability justice within tourism and leisure geographies. Directly addressing the conference theme, “Geographies of Inequality,” this session explores what it truly means to establish tourism and leisure spaces that are not simply compliant, but are genuinely welcoming, equitable, and inclusive of human diversity.

This session aims to bridge a connection between scholarship on embodiment and affect andcritical principles of disability justice. Drawing on this framework that demands not just entry, but also equitable participation, intersectional analysis, and collective liberation, we invite papers that prioritize the knowledge and experiences of disabled people to explore how leisure spaces are navigated, contested, and re-imagined. We are particularly interested in research that moves beyond a singular focus on physical impairment to embrace neurodiversity, sensory worlds, and the complex interplay of multiple social identities.

Potential topics include, but are not limited to:
• Critiques of checklist-style accessibility and the pursuit of belonging, dignity, and joy.
• The sensory, affective, and embodied geographies of navigating, negotiating, or avoiding tourist destinations.
• Intersectional analyses where disability meets race, gender, queerness, and class in leisure contexts.
• Disability activism, community-led tourism, and the co-design of just and inclusive spaces.
• Re-imagining heritage and nature-based tourism through a disability justice lens.
• The role of digital platforms and media in fostering justice.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Gender Inequalities: What Geographic Data Can (and Cannot) Tell Us
schedule_event.description:
Over the past decade, growing attention has been given to gender data gaps across multiple domains. In response, an increasing number of studies have sought to address these gaps and produce more inclusive forms of knowledge strengthening our understanding of existing gender inequalities. Geographic data, in particular, offer powerful tools for understanding the spatial dimensions of gender inequalities and their intersections with other socio-spatial inequalities.

At the same time, despite living in a data-rich society, gender-disaggregated data remain far from standard practice. When available, such data often rely on binary classifications that fail to capture the full complexity of gendered experiences.

This session aims to bring together scholars who use geographic data to examine gender inequalities. We seek to foster discussion around new empirical evidence, unexplored potential, and persistent gaps and limitations in existing data and approaches.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Rural Transformations and Fire
schedule_event.description:
Land use change is widely recognised as a key driver of changing fire regimes (UNEP 2022). Across much of the world, such changes are deeply entangled with ongoing rural transformations that reshape how land is used, managed, inhabited, and governed. In this panel, we ask: how are rural transformations reconfiguring fire regimes in the twenty-first century?

We approach agrarian change as not simply a process of rural abandonment or a shift away from agriculture. Rather, across geographies, it unfolds unevenly, often involving partial and incomplete shifts away from traditional livelihood practices. Agrarian change is better understood as a constellation of transformations in land use, labour relations, market integration, household composition, and rural demography (Li 2014; Bernstein 2021). Contemporary rural transformations may involve livelihood diversification, seasonal migration, new conservation regimes, climate mitigation policies, rewilding initiatives, or intensified commercial agriculture. Together, these shifts reconfigure the social and ecological conditions under which fire burns. Shifts in rural labour through migration and off-farm employment can alter the timing, frequency, and collective coordination of controlled burning (Yadav 2026). With fewer people living and working on the land, everyday fire stewardship may decline, leading in some contexts to fuel accumulation and heightened wildfire risk (Mathews and Malfatti 2024). In others, intensified state regulation, conservation, or carbon-oriented climate interventions redefine legality, marginalise traditional burning practices, and reinforce fire as a problem to be suppressed (Smith, Perkins and Mistry 2022; Eilenberg 2022).

We are particularly interested in how changing configurations of land, labour, and livelihood strategies affect:

  • The continuity or transformation of traditional fire use practices
  • Collective systems of fire management
  • Intergenerational transmission of fire knowledge
  • The capacity of communities and states to respond to unwanted or catastrophic fires
  • The politics of blame, responsibility, and governance in changing rural contexts


We welcome contributions from both the Global North and the Global South that attend to the uneven, messy, and often contradictory micro-processes through which agrarian change unfolds on the ground and how shifting rhythms of rural life are actively reshaping the conditions under which fire is used, suppressed, politicised, or allowed to burn.

References:

Li, Tania Murray (2014) Land’s End: Capitalist Relations on an Indigenous Frontier. Durham, NC: Duke University Press.Bernstein, Henry (2021) Class Dynamics of Agrarian Change. Halifax & Winnipeg: Fernwood Publishing.

Mathews, Andrew S. & Malfatti, Federico (2024) ‘Wildfires as legacies of agropastoral abandonment: Gendered litter raking and managed burning as historic fire prevention practices in the Monte Pisano of Italy’, Ambio, 53, pp. 1065–1076. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13280-024-01993-x

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Contesting Tourism Growth: Critical Geographies, Spatial Inequalities, and Just Alternatives (2)
schedule_event.description:
This panel brings together critical perspectives on tourism growth as a spatially uneven and politically contested process. Across diverse geographical contexts, tourism is frequently positioned as a development solution, yet its expansion often intensifies inequalities in housing, labour, mobility, infrastructure, and access to decision-making—particularly in tourism-dependent and geographically constrained places such as islands, rural regions, and peripheral urban areas.

The panel is structured around two complementary themes that speak to ongoing debates in critical geography, urban studies, and political economy.

1: Urbanisation, Governance, and Spatial Inequality examines how tourism growth is planned, governed, and contested, and how it reshapes urban and regional space. Contributions in this theme explore the governance arrangements, policy frameworks, and institutional dynamics through which tourism-led development produces uneven outcomes, including displacement, housing pressures, precarious labour, and differentiated mobilities. Taken together, the papers and presentations situate tourism within broader processes of urbanisation, territorial restructuring, and spatial injustice.

2: Contestation, Alternatives, and Post-Growth Futures turns attention to challenges to growth-oriented tourism models and to emerging alternatives. Drawing on degrowth, regenerative, post-capitalist, and place-based approaches, this theme foregrounds grassroots, community-led, and Indigenous practices that reimagine tourism beyond extraction and expansion. Papers and presentations will explore how alternative tourism futures are articulated, negotiated, and sometimes institutionalised, and who has the power to define what constitutes a “just” tourism geography across different spatial and political scales.

Together, the panel offers a critical and forward-looking examination of tourism’s role in shaping space, inequality, and possibility. It will be of interest to scholars and practitioners working on urbanisation, governance, political ecology, degrowth, and spatial justice, as well as those seeking to engage with post-growth debates and transformative approaches to tourism and place-making.

This session will be held in-person in at the RGS conference London, with a hybrid option available for 2. This session is sponsored by the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group (GLTRG) at the RGS conference.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Social Infrastructures for Net Zero: Enabling just place-based decarbonisation (1)
schedule_event.description:
As a particular political and economic project, Net Zero prioritises market-based mechanisms and large-scale technological interventions whilst leaving the social infrastructures essential for collective climate action under-resourced. This continues a longer trajectory whereby decades of austerity, privatisation, and the extension of market logics have systematically dismantled the spaces, resources, and time necessary for collective organising—rendering invisible the care work, skill-sharing, and relational practices upon which sustainable transitions fundamentally depend.

This panel challenges these narrow framings by foregrounding social infrastructure as a critical yet marginalised dimension of sustainability transitions. We conceptualise social infrastructure not merely as physical spaces that support collective action, but as relational processes constituted through labour, care, and collective actions. Social infrastructures operate bidirectionally: they enable the collective action necessary for decarbonisation, and are co-produced with Net Zero agendas. Community energy projects, retrofit programmes, and grassroots climate organising all depend upon—and simultaneously create—networks of care, skill-sharing, resource exchange, and collective mobilisation.

The panel explores the multi-scalar relationships between decarbonisation and justice, examining how these operate from household to neighbourhood to national levels. We consider how existing community mobilisations can be supported and enhanced, whilst ensuring that place-based needs and diverse forms of community organising are recognised and resourced. Crucially, we examine: What are the social infrastructures of just, rapid and deep decarbonisation, what enables and sustains it? By making visible the gendered, classed, and racialised relations upon which decarbonisation depends, we advance a more radical approach that centres people, places, and power relations in just transitions.

Speakers in our sessions will share findings from their research (Session 1) and provocations for discussion (Session 2) on the following questions: What are the social infrastructures of just, rapid and deep decarbonisation, what enables and sustains them? Which forms of labour, care and social relations sustain Net Zero infrastructures? Who does the work of Net Zero, under what conditions, and for whom? How do social infrastructures of decarbonisation operate across scales, from households to neighbourhoods, cities, and national policy frameworks? What types of social infrastructure matter most in advancing Net Zero? How is knowledge about decarbonisation produced, shared, and legitimised within and across different social infrastructures?

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Social Infrastructures for Net Zero: Enabling just place-based decarbonisation (2)
schedule_event.description:
This world café discussion continues dialogue from Session 1, inviting a broad audience to share their experiences and reflections on enabling just place-based decarbonisation.

Social infrastructures are vital to the achievement of Net Zero, and are simultaneously co-produced with Net Zero agendas. We conceptualise social infrastructures as relational processes constituted through labour, care, and collective actions. Social infrastructures operate bidirectionally: they enable collective action for decarbonisation, and they are affected by Net Zero policies, funding and activity. Community energy projects, retrofit programmes, sustainable transport movements, community food intiatives and grassroots climate organising all depend upon - and simultaneously create - networks of care, skill-sharing, resource exchange, and collective mobilisation.

Our discussants will share perspectives from different socio-technical systems and geographic settings: 

  1. Jorge Ortiz-Moreno, University of Manchester (water), ‘What forms of care work are needed to advance decarbonisation at the household level? Lessons from the adoption of rainwater harvesting facilities in Mexico’.
  2. Patricia Aelbrecht, Public Space Observatory Research Centre, Geography and Planning School, Cardiff University. ‘What London’s Libraries Teach Us About Social Value and Net Zero Goals’.
  3. Stefan Bouzarovski, Geography, University of Manchester, ‘The hidden labour(s) of social infrastructure’.
  4. Katy Roelich, Sustainability Research Institute, School of Earth, Environment and Sustainability, University of Leeds. ‘What social infrastructure is needed to decarbonise transport?’
  5. Sarah Mander, (community resilience) CAST and Tyndall Centre, University of Manchester. ‘Stronger together: building resilience through community conversations’.

Our facilitators will then convene a World Café discussion to explore the following questions: 

    • What is the social infrastructure of decarbonisation, what enables and sustains it?
    • How do capacities and inequalities shape participation in and/or visibility of social infrastructure?
    • What types of social infrastructure matter most in advancing Net Zero agendas? How should this be cultivated to enable just, place-based decarbonisation?

    We welcome participation from all involved in just place-based decarbonisation activity, whether through research, policy or practice.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Plants and Empires: The Botanical Stories of Critical Geographies of Race and Indigeneity
schedule_event.description: Plants are alive and so is empire. Botanical extractions, repositories, sciences, and speculations were, and continue to be, central to imperial projects and relations. In this session, contributors examine the relationships between plants and empire through creative angles, in particular posing analysis through a critique of the garden. Several papers in this session juxtapose the palatable nature of the garden magazine form with the brutal colonial political economic processes laden within forms of “the garden”. The garden is a valorized cultural form in a white supremacist imaginary of human-environment relations. The garden is a space of recreation, of social reproduction, an outside to/escape from the drudgery of late capitalism. But it is an inescapably imperial form. The garden engendered imperial occupation and dispossession and empire engendered the niceness, valorization, and the aesthetics of the garden as we know it. Through the framing of the “garden”, we examine and unpack the themes: living empires, imperial and decolonial possibilities of botanical collections, and the co-constituted agency of colonized peoples and their plant relations. Contributors in this session draw on methods and approaches from geographies of race, Indigenous geographies, Black geographies and Black ecologies as well as materially-grounded geographical critique. Doing so we offer an examination of large scale processes of political economic transformation and place-specific resistance to those processes by conducting our analyses through specific plants and the aesthetic logics and forms of “the garden.” In this session, contributors touch on some of the following: How did pine trees make much of the movement of British empire possible? How does the cochineal insect that only grows on cactus in a small region of Mexico color and clothe empires the world over? What should we make of ornamental camellias that traveled alongside tea plants on East India Company ships and became quintessential in English gardens? How might we restore Indigenous knowledge and relationships to the plant materials held in drawers and bottles and vials in Kew’s Economic Botany collection, and repurpose them intellectually for de-imperialized futures? This session touches on these questions and much more on the imperial relations of plants and botanical resistances to empire across the earth.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Rural Events Between Community Cohesion and Tourism Mobilities: Rethinking Rurality Through Events
schedule_event.description:
Events are commonly studied within urban contexts, where they are embedded in dense cultural, economic, and social infrastructures. In contrast, events in rural settings—remote, peripheral, or insular—have received significantly less scholarly attention, often framed through deficit-based narratives. This session proposes a critical reconsideration of “rural events” by interrogating the very notion of rurality and its associated stigmas and inequalities. Rather than treating the rural as a simple spatial or demographic descriptor, we explore how rurality is socially constructed, negotiated, and contested, and how events offer a lens for reimagining these processes.

Existing research on rural events frequently emphasises what rural areas “lack”—infrastructure, funding mechanisms, professionalization—risking the reproduction of simplistic stereotypes and overlooking the resilience and innovation exhibited by rural communities. At the same time, rural events are widely recognized as key social, cultural, and political infrastructures, strengthening community cohesion, generating belonging, and supporting local identity. Increasingly, they also intersect with tourism mobilities, becoming drivers of place marketing, economic diversification, and cultural commodification. This duality raises critical questions about how rural events navigate tensions between community-oriented values and tourism-driven agendas, and how such dynamics shape local identities, power relations, and social change.

This session invites contributions that critically examine the complex relationality between events, rurality, community life, and tourism flows. By exploring events through their rurality, we aim to uncover how they sustain community cohesion while simultaneously responding to broader mobilities, demographic shifts, and development and cultural pressures.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Negotiating Dignity: Rethinking Justice in Geographical Research (1)
schedule_event.description:
Papers in this session place dignity at the centre of geographical research on justice. Over the past two decades, justice has become a core concern across multiple subdisciplines, including critical urban geography, environmental and climate justice, feminist and care geographies, Black and anti-racist geographies, political geography, and mobilities and transport geography. Much of this work has productively moved beyond distributive frameworks to address recognition, participation, embodiment, affect, and everyday power (Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). Yet the normative and lived stakes of these debates are often articulated indirectly. Dignity helps name what is frequently at issue but insufficiently theorised: the ability to inhabit space without humiliation, erasure, or disposability, and to exercise autonomy and social standing in the face of structural inequality. At the same time, dignity is a concept with a complex and contested history. Its universalist claims—long embedded in law, bioethics, and humanitarian governance—have often failed to confront colonial, racialised, gendered, and caste-based hierarchies, and at times have actively reproduced them (Misztal, 2013). Recent geographical scholarship has responded by advancing relational, contextual, and politically grounded understandings of dignity, attentive to how it is produced, denied, and negotiated through everyday encounters with institutions, infrastructures, and publics (Grossmann and Trubina, 2022; Kariũki and Martens, 2026; Plyushteva, 2023; McIlwaine et al., 2025). Taken together, dignity-oriented research extends geographical debates on justice. It draws attention to the affective and ethical dimensions of inequality that are often muted in distributive or procedural accounts. In doing so, it highlights how injustice is reproduced through routine practices of degradation and erasure.

References

Grossmann, K., & Trubina, E. (2022). Dignity in urban geography: Starting a conversation. Dialogues in Human Geography, 12(3), 406-426.

Hopkins, P. (2021). Social geography III: Committing to social justice. Progress in Human Geography, 45(2), 382-393.

Kariũki, W., & Martens, K. (2026). Dignified transport: The case of paratransit. Travel Behaviour and Society, 42, 101140.

McIlwaine, C., Peppl, R., & Cal Angrisani, C. (2025). Reclaiming dignity through creative encounters among Brazilian women survivors of violence in London. cultural geographies, 14744740251319039.

Misztal, B. A. (2013). The idea of dignity: Its modern significance. European Journal of Social Theory, 16(1), 101-121.

Plyushteva, A. (2023). Negotiating dignity in public geography: The ethics of public engagement in pandemic times. Area, 55(1), 53-61.

Przybylinski, S. (2022). Where is justice in geography? A review of justice theorizing in the discipline. Geography Compass, 16(3), e12615.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Flipping the script: Exploring the possibilities of applied theatre for bringing Indigenous, vernacular and place-based knowledges into climate change adaptation
schedule_event.description:
Finding meaningful ways to blend scientific knowledges of the physical effects of climate change as measured through tools and modelling advancements, and local knowledges of at-risk communities about the availability and success of place-based adaptation options is an urgent challenge. Hosted by the Climate Collaboratorium Project, this session explores the transformative potential of creative, participatory and emergent approaches such as applied theatre, design thinking, improvisation and futures labs, in supporting place-based responses to climate change adaptation in communities exposed to environmental risks. Bringing together international perspectives, it pays particular attention to situated emergences across a diverse range of local examples.

Keen to amplify and connect the - often hidden - communities, projects and practitioners engaging in interdisciplinary arts-based approaches to climate change adaptation, Flipping the script is designed as a participatory session that shares the framing, processes and practices of work in different settings. We seek contributions from projects working directly with communities, and our aim is to broaden the voices, perspectives and approaches represented in this work. We encourage submissions that involve community voices, creative practitioner and artist perspectives and those that use exploratory, arts-based, interactive and experimental ways of investigating issues and presenting ideas.

Each contribution will have 10 minutes, followed by a creatively facilitated plenary, supporting questions, discussion and new connections.
