schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Designing Pluriversality in Times of Crisis: Envisioning Spatial Justice Through Alternative Cartographies (1)
schedule_event.description:
What happens when crises become mappable?

Contemporary societies are increasingly shaped by crisis as an everyday condition rather than an exceptional event. Environmental contamination, climate risks, infrastructural failures, humanitarian displacements - to mention a few - unfold through a mix of visible emergencies and less visible, slow, and unevenly distributed forms of harm. These crises are not only experienced on the ground; they are continuously identified, stabilised, and rendered governable through visual systems.

Maps, dashboards, and visual models have become central to how crises are recognised, communicated, and acted upon. From pollution alerts and flood forecasts to humanitarian risk maps and urban performance indicators, crisis cartographies translate complex, uncertain, and often contested conditions into spatial forms that can circulate across institutions, policy arenas, and publics. In doing so, they do more than depict crises. They shape how crisis is defined, where it is located, whose experiences are counted, and which responses appear legitimate or necessary.

These mapping practices are deeply implicated in the production and governance of inequality. Crisis cartographies rely on classificatory schemes, thresholds, and visual conventions that privilege certain forms of knowledge and render others marginal or invisible. While often mobilised in the name of evidence-based decision-making, dominant visual approaches can flatten lived experiences, obscure historical responsibility, and reproduce epistemic and spatial injustices.

We approach mapping as a political, ethical, and relational practice rather than a neutral technique. The session highlights how cartographies operate not only as representations but also as methods, infrastructures, and interventions that influence how crises and inequalities are understood and addressed. It asks how geographers might rethink mapping practices to support more just and plural spatial futures, within and beyond institutional frameworks.

We welcome empirical, conceptual, and methodological papers engaging with themes including:

• Crisis cartographies as visual objects, examining how maps, dashboards, and visual models shape what is seen, valued, and governed

• Crisis cartographies as methods, focusing on mapping as a process of sensing, translating, and maintaining crisis data and experiences, including relational or low tech approaches

• Crisis cartographies as interventions, exploring how mapping participates in political struggles, institutional decision making, or collective action

• Crisis cartographies as infrastructures, attending to the socio technical systems, platforms, policies, and labour that sustain mapping practices over time

• How mapping practices shape the recognition and governance of inequality, and whose experiences are made visible or left out

• Alternative cartographic practices, such as participatory, feminist, decolonial, artistic, or speculative mapping, that rework dominant visual orders and open possibilities for spatial justice.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - From Inequalities to Injustices: Perinatal Health and the Experience of Place
schedule_event.description:
This panel examines how the study of perinatal inequalities can take form through the lens of geographies of dwelling and livelihood (Ingold, 2000). It approaches socio-spatial inequalities through processes of spatial appropriation linked to residential precarity, as well as through the logics of sorting and fragmentation of public policies, which directly produce vulnerability, particularly among women, gender minorities, and more broadly marginalized groups. By adopting a population-based approach focused on so-called “vulnerable” or “precarious” individuals and on their spatial practices, the panel analyzes the social production of inequalities as it materializes in forms of constraints on dwelling and spatial invisibilization.

The French territory provides a particularly fertile ground for critical reflection within the geography of inequalities, as it clearly reveals the close relationship between socio-spatial inequalities and deteriorating health outcomes. Maternal mortality rates illustrate this pattern: the Paris metropolitan area records the highest rate in mainland France, while former French colonies that have been departmentalised experience rates several times higher (Deneux-Tharaux & Saucedo, 2024). An institutional context structured around “fragmentation and temporariness as a regulatory norm of poverty” (Gardella, 2016), combined with the saturation of reception facilities, generates a genuine “geography of wandering” for individuals and families facing housing-related precarity (Iasagkasvili, 2025).

Research has shown that care work generates health inequalities that may extend to violence against minoritized and racialized groups (Crear-Perry, J., et al. 2021.). These inequalities also affect care providers themselves, particularly medical and paramedical professionals. Through task delegation processes, institutions frequently assign them to so-called “dirty work,” thereby reproducing structural inequalities within the labor market (Avril & Ramos Vacca, 2020). These inequalities become embodied in places and people, reflecting how structural policies create what Davis (2019) calls 'reproductive stratification', therefore determining whose reproduction is valued and supported, and whose is constrained or endangered.

Finally, the panel explores the experiences of “affected” researchers (Favret-Saada, 1990) who adopt a dual positioning as doctoral researchers and healthcare professionals. By conducting research within their own work context, these researchers occupy a situated position that requires sustained observation and prolonged interaction while directly participating in “ordinary representations, routine practices, and their emic meanings” (Olivier de Sardan, 1995). Rather than adhering to approaches that claim researcher neutrality (Bernardie-Tahir & Schmoll, 2012), these experiences actively contribute to debates on the geographies of inequality and health, as well as on their role in the production of spaces of (in)justice (Soja, 2013).

schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Producing Marginality in Motion: Questions of ‘Social Justice’ in the Public Transport System of the Global South
schedule_event.description:
Public transport is often viewed as a key component of urban mobility. But its needless to mention that there has been a declining trend in its modal share globally. In developing countries inadequate and poor public transport infrastructure due to lack of investment by local or national government, high cost of fuel, poorly connected road-network have been encouraging rapid transformation of urban mobility vis-à-vis consumers’ preference for timely, hassle-free direct travel. The complex interaction between sprawled urban growth and transport connectivity have fueled the enormous growth of private transport facilities. As observed in other developing countries, in India also rise in car ownership has not been significant. Rather car rental services and later internet-based ride hailing services became extremely popular among a major section of the population. In spite of its importance as a suitable multimodal travel option for sustainable urban development, recent research have been indicating that such services have been causing social exclusion, aggravating inequalities, particularly among low-income groups, Deb 2021, Young 2022, Oviedo et al 2025. Poorer sections of the population who live in the fringe areas with weaker transport facilities have been paying more to reach their workplace than the urban dwellers, Hine & Mitchell 2001. In emerging economies, such internet-based services have made urbanization-induced employment opportunities easily accessible to young generations, but the perceived risks are often overlooked, Mageto & Luke 2025. From a broader perspective certain ‘costs’ of such services rarely get estimated as significant factors, so candidly in the daily struggle of the young commuters.

At this backdrop, this paper aims to conceptualize the idea of ‘poverty’ as an exacerbation of ‘marginalisation’; resulting from inequalities in everyday urban mobility due to the growing popularity of ride-hailing services in Kolkata.

Based on experiences among commuters of varying age and socioeconomic backgrounds, the paper explores the interrelation between urbanization, transport disadvantage and transport poverty as experienced in everyday life. This remains an understudied issue. There is an urgent need to bring forth these aspects of ‘marginalisation’ as pertinent issues often unaddressed in mainstream sustainable mobility planning.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Contesting Tourism Growth: Critical Geographies, Spatial Inequalities, and Just Alternatives (1)
schedule_event.description:
This panel brings together critical perspectives on tourism growth as a spatially uneven and politically contested process. Across diverse geographical contexts, tourism is frequently positioned as a development solution, yet its expansion often intensifies inequalities in housing, labour, mobility, infrastructure, and access to decision-making—particularly in tourism-dependent and geographically constrained places such as islands, rural regions, and peripheral urban areas.

The panel is structured around two complementary themes that speak to ongoing debates in critical geography, urban studies, and political economy.

1: Urbanisation, Governance, and Spatial Inequality examines how tourism growth is planned, governed, and contested, and how it reshapes urban and regional space. Contributions in this theme explore the governance arrangements, policy frameworks, and institutional dynamics through which tourism-led development produces uneven outcomes, including displacement, housing pressures, precarious labour, and differentiated mobilities. Taken together, the papers and presentations situate tourism within broader processes of urbanisation, territorial restructuring, and spatial injustice.

Panel B: Contestation, Alternatives, and Post-Growth Futures turns attention to challenges to growth-oriented tourism models and to emerging alternatives. Drawing on degrowth, regenerative, post-capitalist, and place-based approaches, this theme foregrounds grassroots, community-led, and Indigenous practices that reimagine tourism beyond extraction and expansion. Papers and presentations will explore how alternative tourism futures are articulated, negotiated, and sometimes institutionalised, and who has the power to define what constitutes a “just” tourism geography across different spatial and political scales.

Together, the panel offers a critical and forward-looking examination of tourism’s role in shaping space, inequality, and possibility. It will be of interest to scholars and practitioners working on urbanisation, governance, political ecology, degrowth, and spatial justice, as well as those seeking to engage with post-growth debates and transformative approaches to tourism and place-making.

This session will be held in-person in at the RGS conference London, with a hybrid option available for 2.  This session is sponsored by the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group (GLTRG) at the RGS conference.
